news_communities.title: Lynn celebrates disability awareness with flag raising
news_communities.author: Olivia HaydarOlivia HaydarOlivia Haydar
news_communities.summary:
LYNN — Nothing was going to stop Lynn Disability Network from raising a pride flag in celebration of the 36th anniversary of the Americans with Disabilities Act; not even the monstrous rainstorm that rang thunder and lightning around City Hall. 

Originally planned to be outside before the rain came pouring down, the celebration took place inside City Hall’s foyer on Tuesday evening. 

The founder of Lynn Disability Network (LDN), Louise Dominique, shared that no matter what the weather turned out to be, there would be absolutely no cancellation. 

Many city and state officials joined the celebration to share how large LDN’s impact is on community members, altering their lives in the most positive way possible. This makes those who often get overlooked seen, heard, and understood. 

Lynn School Committee member Tristan Smith expressed how influential Dominique has been in creating opportunities for children and adults with disabilities. 

“She deserves everyone’s praise for all her work across the city, not limited to her work to put tonight together,” Smith said. 

Dominique’s motivation for everything she does has been her son Nathan, a 13-year-old teenager with autism. 

“When I see my son, I see everything,” Dominique said. 

Mayor Jared C. Nicholson expressed his immense gratitude to Dominique and her efforts in creating more inclusiveness for people with disabilities in the community, congratulating her leadership in making Lynn a more accessible space for all people.  

Massachusetts Sen. Brendan Crighton echoed Nicholson’s gratitude, sharing that LDN represents exactly what the City of Lynn does. 

“They continue by our nature,” Crighton said. “Community, accessible, ethical, and beauty.” 

Lynn Disability Commission (LDC) Chair Richelle Waterman-Williams shared how this level of awareness is not just a day of celebration. It is a day, a week, a month, a year, a lifetime of keeping awareness on the forefront of their minds. 

“If you don’t have anybody to work with, we have the voices, the passion, and the dedication to keep up the awareness for this work,” Waterman-Williams said. 

The chairperson for the Swampscott Disability Commission, Lauren Shrestha, was also in attendance for the celebration, sharing that all people deserve accessibility, especially people with disabilities. 

“Accessibility isn’t something you can build in a day,” Shrestha said. “We have to continue to work towards it every single day… Whether we’re talking about accessible parks or transportation for communities, every effort sends the same message; we belong here.” 

Former State Rep. Jamie Belsito, who is also running for Congress, shared how thankful she is to be speaking as a mother and how encouraging it is to see a mother like Dominique do so much for the community. 

“It’s about being here for each other, and I’m just grateful to be here in this way,” Belsito said. 

Considering the weather outside, the flag was drawn open with all of its colors inside the City Hall foyer by Dominique, friends, and even City Councilor Cardeliz Paez. 

Smith shared that the flag’s colors hold significance: the red stripe represents physical disabilities, the gold stripe represents neurodivergence and cognitive disabilities, white represents invisible and undiagnosed disabilities, blue represents psychiatric and mental health disabilities, green represents sensory disabilities like blindness, deafness and sensory processing, and the black background represents all of the grief and rage for those lost to ableism, institutional neglect and violence. 

The colors are not the only symbolism, however. The stripes of colors on the flag are jagged and diagonal, symbolizing breaking free of societal barriers that have segregated disabled people in the past. 

With divine intervention after the flag was displayed in the foyer, the rain and thunderstorm let up with only a drizzle, keeping all dangers of electrocution away, allowing all city officials and Dominique to raise the flag on the City Hall flag pole.

After the flag stood tall, attendees joined together in conversation and feasted on pizza and a 36-year Americans with Disabilities Act birthday cake. 

For more information about the Lynn Disability Network, navigate to their Facebook page, and for more information about the Lynn Disability Commission, navigate to the City of Lynn website. 

The post Lynn celebrates disability awareness with flag raising appeared first on Itemlive.

news_communities.title: Massachusetts ballot will ask nine questions
news_communities.author: Sophia HarrisSophia HarrisSophia Harris
news_communities.summary:
Massachusetts voters will decide nine statewide ballot questions in the Nov. 3 election after the Secretary of the Commonwealth’s Elections Division announced Tuesday the order in which the measures will appear on the ballot.

The ballot question numbers were assigned after all statewide initiatives and referendums were certified. According to the Secretary of the Commonwealth’s Office, the order was based primarily on the length of each question’s summary to make the most efficient use of ballot space.

The statewide ballot will feature the most questions Massachusetts voters have seen since 1994. Because of that, election officials expect ballots in most communities to be two to three pages long. The final length will depend on the number of candidates, local ballot questions, and whether ballots must be printed in multiple languages.

A plain-language summary of each question, based on information released by the Secretary of the Commonwealth’s Office and Ballotpedia, is below.

Question 1: Expanding the Public Records Law

This proposal would expand the state’s public records law to cover the governor’s office and the Massachusetts Legislature, which are currently exempt from many of the law’s requirements. If approved, members of the public would gain broader access to records held by those offices, although some documents would remain exempt under existing public records protections.

Question 2: Collective Bargaining for CPCS Employees

The measure would give employees of the Committee for Public Counsel Services, the state agency that oversees legal representation for people who cannot afford an attorney, the right to collectively bargain. That would allow eligible employees, including public defenders and support staff, to negotiate wages, benefits and working conditions through a union.

Question 3: State Primary Elections

Rather than holding separate Democratic and Republican primaries, this proposal would create a single statewide primary ballot listing every candidate regardless of party affiliation. The two candidates receiving the most votes would advance to the general election, even if they belong to the same political party.

Question 4: Election Day Registration

Massachusetts currently requires voters to register before Election Day. This proposal would allow eligible residents to register and cast a ballot on the same day, eliminating the current registration deadline for those choosing to vote in person.

Question 5: State Revenue Limit & Rebate

The initiative would place a cap on the amount of tax revenue the state could keep each year. If state collections exceeded that limit, the excess money would be returned to taxpayers through rebates rather than remaining in state government accounts.

Question 6: Natural Resource Conservation Fund

The measure would establish the Nature for All Fund, a dedicated account for conservation projects. It would direct revenue from existing sales taxes on certain outdoor recreation-related purchases, including sporting goods and recreational vehicles, toward protecting natural resources, expanding outdoor recreation opportunities and supporting environmental projects.

Question 7: Single Family Homes

This proposal is aimed at increasing housing opportunities by requiring cities and towns to allow single-family homes on residential lots that are at least 5,000 square feet and meet other applicable local requirements. Supporters say it would make it easier to build homes in communities with restrictive zoning rules.

Question 8: Prohibit Retail Sale of Adult Recreational Use Marijuana

If approved, this measure would end the licensed retail sale of recreational marijuana in Massachusetts by repealing portions of the 2016 voter-approved legalization law. The proposal would continue to allow adults to possess limited amounts of marijuana for personal use.

Question 9: Firearms Regulation

The final question is a referendum on the state’s 2024 firearms law, An Act Modernizing Firearm Laws. Voters will decide whether to keep the law in effect or repeal it through the ballot.

First Deputy Secretary Michelle Tassinari, director of the Elections Division, assigned the ballot question numbers.

The Secretary of the Commonwealth’s Office plans to publish its official voter information guide in September. The guide will include the full text of each question, summaries, arguments for and against the measures, and other election information ahead of the Nov. 3 election.

The post Massachusetts ballot will ask nine questions appeared first on Itemlive.

news_communities.title: Mayor Michelle Wu, US Army Corps of Engineers release draft plan to protect against sea-level rise
news_communities.author: admin
news_communities.summary:
City: Coastal infrastructure projects would help protect the over 40% of Boston residents at risk of flooding and prevent over $40 billion in economic loss by end of century

Today Mayor Michelle Wu announced the release of the US Army Corps of Engineers’ (USACE) draft Boston Coastal Storm Risk Management Feasibility Study, a once-in-a-generation study to protect Boston against coastal flooding and sea level rise. This study, projected to be completed by 2028, will allow the City of Boston to access up to 65% federal cost-sharing to construct coastal resilience projects to prevent an estimated $41 billion in damages from coastal flooding.

The proposed projects outlined in the draft report are open to public comment from July 24 through September 18. Residents and stakeholders can submit their feedback and learn about upcoming community meetings and webinars at boston.gov/flood-prevention.

“Boston leads with bold action to protect our communities and our future,” said Mayor Michelle Wu. “The future of our city depends on the steps we take now to build resilient communities. With more frequent flooding in vulnerable areas during extreme weather events, the Coastal Storm Risk Management Feasibility Study is critical to understanding how to protect inland neighborhoods most at risk and unlocking resources to take action. I’m grateful for all our partners working to ensure Boston is resilient, prepared, and beautiful.”

“Today marks a major milestone in our shared mission of community resilience,” said Lieutenant Colonel David MacPhail, Deputy Commander of the New England District of the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers. “The reality is that doing nothing is not a viable option. Without action, the costs to public safety and economic vitality from coastal flooding will only be compounded.”

The project would allow Boston to receive up to 65% federal funding for construction through the USACE partnership, with the remaining costs to be covered through state, local, and private funding sources. The draft plan builds on years of Climate Ready Boston planning and neighborhood resilience studies.

The post Mayor Michelle Wu, US Army Corps of Engineers release draft plan to protect against sea-level rise first appeared on EastBoston.com.

news_communities.title: Swampscott resident’s appeal denied on ’40 Steps’ obstruction
news_communities.author: Erin HickeyErin HickeyErin Hickey
news_communities.summary:
SWAMPSCOTT — The Zoning Board of Appeals unanimously rejected a resident’s challenge to a nearly completed addition at 24-28 Ingalls Terrace, concluding that its role was limited to determining whether the project was being built according to approved plans. 

Tom Bogacz appeared before the board to speak on his appeal concerning Building Commissioner Richard Baldacci’s determination that the project complies with the town’s zoning bylaw, arguing the addition unnecessarily obstructs views of Swampscott Harbor from the public stairway known as the “40 Steps Path.”

The appeal centered on a provision of the town’s site plan review regulations requiring projects to “minimize obstruction of scenic views from publicly accessible locations.” 

The project in question was approved by the Planning Board in January and will preserve and rehabilitate an existing single-family home while adding approximately 2,000 square feet to create a two-family townhouse-style residence. 

Before Bogacz began his presentation, Zoning Board of Appeals Chair Heather Roman emphasized that the board’s review would need to be narrowly defined and that they were unable to revisit the decision on the plans that were approved in January. 

“Our jurisdiction isn’t to go back to the original site plan review,” Roman said. “This is strictly whether the building somehow differs from what was approved by the Planning Board.” 

During his presentation, Bogacz presented photographs, architectural drawings, zoning documents, and measurements that he said showed the project as constructed failed to satisfy the scenic-view requirement. 

Bogacz argued that while the height measurements in the original plan may not have changed, that the building’s roof mass occupied a significantly larger portion of the harbor view than what was represented during the Planning Board process. He also questioned whether other aspects of the project, including setbacks, open-space calculations, and a third-floor configuration complied with the approved plans. 

Bogacz said that during the Planning Board’s site plan review process, the structure was not shown from the perspective of the public path, and he wondered if the added context of the view obstruction warranted a change in the plans. 

“I’m not asking the board to redesign the project,” Bogacz said. “I’m simply asking to evaluate this project against the actual scenic backdrop to determine if it actually does comply with the bylaw.” 

In June, Baldacci determined the project was being built in substantial compliance with the approved plans, writing that any apparent differences resulted from field conditions, including the property’s topography. During the meeting, Baldacci and members of the ZBA noted that an as-built survey would be done to verify compliance with measurements once the project was complete. 

Attorney Kenneth Schutzer attended the meeting and represented the property’s owner. Schutzer argued the board had only one legal question before it: whether the building deviated from the plans approved in January. 

Schutzer noted that the Planning Board unanimously approved the project and that no appeal had been filed to that decision within the necessary time period, and that the building permit had similarly gone unchallenged. 

“The record has to be set straight,” Schutzer said. “Unless you hear anything to the contrary from the building commissioner … this board doesn’t have the authority to do anything other than adopt the building commissioner’s determination.” 

Schutzer also noted, though he said that he did not represent the owner at the time, that the Planning Board had considered the scenic obstruction bylaw during their review and that it was only one of several balancing priorities taken into consideration throughout the process. 

Several residents spoke during public comment or submitted comments before the meeting. Those comments reflected a broader debate over how the town can balance increased housing production with preservation of Swampscott’s waterfront character. 

Several residents spoke in support of Bogacz’s appeal, including Sharon Donovan, who said she appreciated the effort to rehabilitate the deteriorating historic house, but that she worried the town was losing one of its defining public vistas. 

“When you get to the top of the steps, and it’s gone, it’s gone,” Donovan said. “What’s the point of having these parameters to guide us, to keep what makes Swampscott special?” 

ZBA member Marc Kornitzky acknowledged Donovan’s concerns, saying he agreed that preserving Swampscott’s character is important. But he reaffirmed that the board’s authority in this case was limited. 

“I agree with you on that 100%,” Kornitsky told Donovan. “We’re not in a position to second-guess the Planning Board. We’re only in a position to see if there’s been compliance with the approved plans.” 

Kornitsky moved to deny the appeal, explaining that the board conducted an independent review of the evidence but found no proof that the project had deviated from what had previously been approved. 

The board voted 5-0 to deny the appeal. 

After the vote, Kornitsky informed Bogacz that once the written decision is filed with the town clerk, he will have the right to seek further review through the courts if he chooses to continue challenging the decision. 

The post Swampscott resident’s appeal denied on ’40 Steps’ obstruction appeared first on Itemlive.

news_communities.title: Peabody’s Olio introduces weekday package for budget-conscious couples
news_communities.author: Amanda LureyAmanda LureyAmanda Lurey
news_communities.summary:
PEABODY — Downtown event venue Olio has introduced a new, all-inclusive package built around affordability, convenience, and intimate gatherings.

Dubbed the Matinee Package, the new offering reimagines the traditional wedding experience by combining nearly every major expense into one flat fee. Designed for up to 50 guests and available on Mondays, Tuesdays, and Wednesdays, the package costs $16,300 and includes the venue, planning, catering, open bar, photography, DJ, officiant, florals, and decor.

Owner Sarah Narcus said the idea came after watching couples become increasingly cautious about spending over the past two years. The package reflects what Narcus calls the rise of the “smart luxury” couple: people who still want a beautiful, high-end wedding but are placing greater value on financial flexibility, intentional celebrations, and a less stressful planning process.

For Olio, that meant simplifying the experience.

Instead of spending months researching vendors, comparing prices, and coordinating timelines, couples book one package that includes an established team of professionals. The only major decisions left are personal touches, like attire and invitations.

“This is going to be a really nice fit for people who want an easy button,” Narcus said. “Like, ‘OK, just plan my wedding for me.’”

The weekday format also opens the door for couples who don’t want to wait years for a coveted Saturday wedding date.

Located inside the restored former theater at 43 Main St., Olio has hosted weddings and events since 2019, blending the building’s historic architecture with modern event design. The venue will continue offering its traditional, customizable wedding packages alongside the new option.

Narcus said recent interest in the package has been encouraging, with tours already underway as couples explore a wedding option that trades excess for ease.

“What I’m finding is that people are really, really grateful just to know that they don’t have to spend their whole year’s salary… and can still have a fun, complete wedding,” she said.

The post Peabody’s Olio introduces weekday package for budget-conscious couples appeared first on Itemlive.

news_communities.title: Backpacks top wish list in Peabody
news_communities.author: Amanda LureyAmanda LureyAmanda Lurey
news_communities.summary:
PEABODY — As families begin preparing for the new school year, Citizens Inn and No Child Goes Hungry in Peabody are once again asking the community to help ensure local students have the supplies they need to start the year with confidence.

The annual school supply drive collects classroom essentials for children served by Citizens Inn, with additional supplies distributed through No Child Goes Hungry in Peabody to reach even more families. The organizations have partnered on the effort for nearly a decade, building on a longstanding collaboration that also includes food distribution.

“Citizens Inn, we’re focused on housing and food security, but we know that when people are experiencing food insecurity or housing insecurity, that’s not going to be their only need, right?” Citizens Inn Executive Director Melissa González said. “People’s needs are complex. … We know that, for example, the families that come to our pantry for food have many other needs, and we want to set kids up for success, and that means that they’re not going hungry, but that also means that we want to make sure that they can start the school year with the supplies that they need.”

Jarrod Hochman, vice president and chief operating officer of No Child Goes Hungry in Peabody, said the goal is to remove one more barrier to learning.

“What we hope to get out of it is healthy, well-rounded kids that don’t feel less than, that don’t feel that they don’t have the proper tools to access all that school offers. … There’s too many roadblocks or impediments in the way for life and school, and kids shouldn’t need to realize those roadblocks because they don’t have the ability, or the family doesn’t have the ability, to provide them with notebooks or pencils or crayons,” Hochman said.

He added that he wants students “to thrive and succeed to the best of their ability.”

Both organizations emphasized that new backpacks are the greatest need, as they are the most expensive item to provide. Other items that can be donated include: glue sticks, markers and crayons, blue and black pens, erasers, No. 2 pencils, colored pencils, pencil sharpeners, pencil cases, highlighters, notebooks, calculators, hand sanitizer, tissues, rulers, folders, loose leaf paper, 3-ring binders, notebooks, and index cards.

Any remaining school supplies are donated to Peabody Public Schools for classroom supply closets.

Donations can be dropped off at dozens of locations through Aug. 5.

In Peabody, donations can be brought to: Brooksby Village, Brother’s Deli, Citizens Inn, Community Covenant Church, Gaetas Towing, GraVoc, No Child Goes Hungry in Peabody, North Shore Children’s Museum, North Suburban Jewish Community Center, Peabody Fire Department, Peabody Police Department, all three branches of the Peabody Library, Sandmagination, Temple Ner Tamid, Temple Tiferet Shalom, Treadwell’s Ice Cream, and more.

The post Backpacks top wish list in Peabody appeared first on Itemlive.

news_communities.title: A two-wheeled memorial on the Lynnway
news_communities.author: Olivia HaydarOlivia HaydarOlivia Haydar
news_communities.summary:
LYNN — A Salem resident couldn’t stop thinking about the fatal tractor-trailer crash on the Lynnway on June 5, so she created a memorial to honor the victim.  

William Beard was killed at approximately 11 a.m. right before the traffic light at the Commercial Street intersection while heading northbound when a tractor-trailer struck and ran him over. 

The Essex County District Attorney’s investigation into the crash is still ongoing. 

Since the crash took place, Ellen Simpson has taken the initiative to craft what’s known as a “ghost bike” in memory of Beard. Ghost bikes are roadside memorials that commemorate cyclist deaths. 

After seeing many in other areas of Massachusetts, Simpson took it upon herself to make one. 

“I do remember seeing my first one and finding it just a really powerful way to speak for people who can no longer speak for themselves,” Simpson said. “I never considered doing one until I learned about the death of Mr. Beard, because that is a place that I drive by most days on my way to Wonderland.” 

The process of creating the bike included purchasing a $20 scrap bike from Facebook Marketplace and giving it a new look with white spray paint and artificial bouquets that will withstand all weather, Simpson said. With the help of a friend, Simpson was also able to create a placard with Beard’s name and day of death. 

“I know sometimes flowers do end up there kind of organically from people who know the victim… but I just wanted a couple of bunches of flowers that would be permanent,” Simpson said. 

While going to place the decorated bicycle at the site of the crash, Simpson noticed flowers were already there. 

With two feet of sidewalk and no buffer zone between the road and pedestrians, Simpson realized a danger residents must face on a daily basis – how close the fast cars really are. 

“People can go very fast on that street, and legally it’s not a street where you go slowly,” Simpson said. “It actually felt super dangerous not being protected from the cars that were whizzing by.” 

Simpson said she hopes a buffer zone between the sidewalk and the roadway will eventually be added to better protect pedestrians. 

Bringing attention to the severe dangers cyclists and pedestrians face on busy roads like the Lynnway, and being proactive in efforts to make commuters’ lives safer, are two of the three solid motivations that drove Simpson to create the ghost bike memorial, alongside commemorating Beard’s death. 

For Simpson, cyclist and pedestrian safety strikes close to home, with three close family members using those transportation modes to get to work every day. 

“(They) really motivate me to make it a safer environment for everyone in our region,” Simpson said. 

The discussion of road safety for cyclists has long been a two-sided issue in Simpson’s eyes, with some taking the side of the bikers, and some taking the side of the drivers, but she hopes for more people to understand that both parties involved in incidents like Beard’s should be given grace. 

“I think we can all align on wanting to be able to use whatever mode of transportation is part of our lives and doing it in a safe way,” Simpson added. 

With the upcoming Lynnway Multimodal Corridor Project, lanes will be reduced to make way for updated bus and bike lanes, an addition of at least two through-lanes in each direction and all existing left-turn lanes, updated sidewalks including 34 new ADA-compliant ramps, and increasing visibility for pedestrians on crosswalks, according to the Massachusetts Bay Transportation Authority (MBTA) website. 

Simpson believes and hopes this project will be mostly positive, making it clear where cyclists and drivers belong on the road. She believes the more signals drivers have to make way for cyclists, the safer it will be. 

While leaving the memorial site, Simpson noticed a family with a child crossing the road during a green light, which piqued her interest even more about the timing of crosswalks, and reducing the pedestrians’ temptation to skip the safety protocol and run across the six lanes. 

“I think they had been waiting a while, and they finally just waited until there was a break in the traffic and they ran across,” Simpson said. “I understand that temptation, and I don’t blame them one bit… but if you make it so that when you press the walk signal, you don’t have to wait very long, then people are more apt to wait.” 

As Simpson worked on Beard’s memorial, two more cyclists were killed in separate crashes in Massachusetts. One was 36-year-old Louisa Gag, who was killed on July 9 in Boston on her commute to City Hall after a collision with a truck. The other was 74-year-old woman Denise Olson, who was killed on July 13 in Dartmouth after a collision with a dump truck. 

Simpson hopes that both of the individuals will receive ghost bike memorials like hers to Beard. 

“I think the point of all of this is to never have to make ghost bikes, but we’re not there yet,” Simpson said.

The post A two-wheeled memorial on the Lynnway appeared first on Itemlive.

news_communities.title: Commentary: Brace yourself for the AI public relations blitz
news_communities.author: Guest CommentaryGuest CommentaryGuest Commentary
news_communities.summary:
J.B. Branch

For years, Silicon Valley executives warned artificial intelligence would transform society, replace jobs, and perhaps pose existential risks. They built products grounded in what should be considered the largest copyright infringement of human history. They fired their own coders who helped build their AI while warning more firings were to come — not just in tech but throughout all industries. They told artists, writers, and musicians that machines trained on their cultural contributions will outperform them.

And then something unexpected happened. The American public didn’t cheer.

In offices across the U.S., workers are hesitant to use AI products they are being told will soon do their jobs better. Medical professionals scoff at the idea that an AI-enabled machine could offer better patient care. People refuse to choose sycophantic AI companions that in some cases have encouraged self-harm.

While some AI products have proved fun or useful, Americans are increasingly suspicious of the technology as a whole.

Americans aren’t irrationally afraid of innovation. We still line up for the newest incrementally updated iPhone. The public also isn’t resistant to AI because it doesn’t get it. The mistrust Big Tech has come to realize has calcified over years of abuse. This is a problem of the companies’ own doing. Poll after poll shows Americans are not buying the AI hype. Moreover, the public is sending a message to the wannabe AI oligarchs: You’ve made your bed; now sit in it.

Big Tech has a major accountability and responsibility problem. But the tech bros see it as nothing more than a serious public relations problem. So get ready for the rehabilitation campaign.

Meta is launching a workforce academy to train tradespeople to build the data centers required for its AI ambitions. Anthropic is creating Claude Corps, almost assuredly named to invoke positive connotations with public fellowships such as AmeriCorps or the Peace Corps, placing young people in nonprofits to spread AI adoption. OpenAI and Microsoft are partnering to train teachers to use AI.

Each announcement arrives wrapped in the language of social good as if to scream, “Big Tech wants to give back!”

But Americans shouldn’t buy that narrative. You cannot spend two decades building social media platforms that fuel misinformation, destabilize institutions, and harm children, and then expect approval for a new technology that will hyperscale these problems. You cannot train systems on humanity’s books, journalism, music, and art without permission and expect creators to celebrate. You cannot tell people their jobs are at risk, lay off workers, replace customer service with chatbots, and expect standing ovations. And you certainly cannot spend years treating regulation as a threat, suddenly position yourself as society’s trusted guide into your ideal future while evading accountability, and then act bewildered when the public rejects your God-machine.

What makes this moment remarkable is how out of touch these tech barons are. The relationship between Silicon Valley and the public is purely extractive. Their wealth doesn’t feed into Main Street; it is sent almost exclusively to California and Wall Street. The AI business model is about as selfish and inhumane of a setup as possible.

That’s not hyperbole. OpenAI CEO Sam Altman’s words describe the gobsmacking greed and disdain Big Tech has toward the basic principles of humanity: “People talk about how much energy it takes to train an AI model — but it also takes a lot of energy to train a human. It takes about 20 years of life — and all the food you consume during that time — before you become smart.”

Tech CEOs have fundamentally destroyed any grace the public was willing to lend. Americans see their business model extracting data, attention, culture, and natural resources that are needed to survive, all for its own profit. The benefits only flow upward to billionaires while communities absorb the costs. That is why the public remains unconvinced.

Meta’s training academy may help some workers. Anthropic’s fellowships may support nonprofits. OpenAI may help some teachers use ChatGPT. But none of that will erase the public’s realization that AI is being built for companies rather than for people.

No fellowship, academy, or glossy public opinion campaign can repair what years of extraction and broken promises have eroded. Silicon Valley spent years teaching the public what it values. Now it is asking to be believed.

But trust, once squandered, is far harder to build than the next model update.

J.B. Branch is director of federal AI governance and technology policy at Public Citizen; he leads the nonprofit organization’s federal advocacy on artificial intelligence, technology policy, and consumer protection. He is a term member of the Council on Foreign Relations and an affiliate of the Harvard Berkman Klein Center for Internet & Society.

The post Commentary: Brace yourself for the AI public relations blitz appeared first on Itemlive.

news_communities.title: AWG announces August exhibition:  We Find Ourselves in Shape of Home, Victoria “thirteenvic” DelValle
news_communities.author: admin
news_communities.summary:
August 1 – August 29, 2026
Opening Reception: Saturday, August 1, 4-7pm
 Third Thursday Reception and Artist Talk: August 20, 6-9pm

(EAST BOSTON- July 21, 2026) Atlantic Works Gallery is pleased to present “We Find Ourselves in Shape of Home”, on view August 1 through August 29, 2026. This exhibit will showcase East Boston artist, Victoria “thirteenvic” DelValle as a guest artist in August.

“We Find Ourselves in the Shape of Home” by artist Victoria “Thirteenvic” DelValle,is a collection of drawings and paintings exploring the cultural preservation of the Puerto Rican diaspora through architecture and community design; how syncretism is intentionally utilized and why hybridity and merging have shaped material language, structural elements, and public space within Villa Victoria (in Boston’s South End neighborhood), in ways that seem simultaneously familiar, grotesque and liminal.

On display at the Atlantic Works Gallery in East Boston, this exhibition connects to the neighborhood through its exploration of migration, diaspora, cultural preservation, and the way identity becomes embedded within urban space. Like Villa Victoria, East Boston is shaped by immigrant communities whose histories, values, and presence are reflected through the neighborhood’s architecture, public spaces, signage, murals, and everyday cultural traces.

Regular gallery hours throughout the exhibition are Fridays and Saturdays from 2:00 PM to 6:00 PM, or by appointment. For more information, please visit www.atlanticworks.org or follow the gallery on Instagram @awgallery. Atlantic Works Gallery, 80 Border Street, East Boston, MA – Free and open to the public

The post AWG announces August exhibition:  We Find Ourselves in Shape of Home, Victoria “thirteenvic” DelValle first appeared on EastBoston.com.

news_communities.title: NeighborHealth Appoints Maria Coppenrath as Chief Financial Officer
news_communities.author: admin
news_communities.summary:
Finance strategy leader with deep Massachusetts community health expertise to join NeighborHealth

REVERE, Mass. (July 21, 2026) — NeighborHealth today announced the appointment of Maria Coppenrath, CPA, as the organization’s new Vice President and Chief Financial Officer, effective July 20, 2026.

With more than 30 years of healthcare finance experience – including over 15 years serving as CFO within complex, multi-site Federally Qualified Health Centers (FQHCs) – Coppenrath brings deep expertise in healthcare finance strategy, value-based care, 340B pharmacy operations, ACO models, and FQHC regulatory compliance.

Most recently, Coppenrath served as CFO at Caring Health Center in Springfield, Massachusetts. She has also held senior financial leadership roles at Greater Lawrence Family Health Center and Edward M. Kennedy Community Health Center, where she led financial transformation initiatives, strengthened organizational sustainability, and supported significant operational growth.

Coppenrath succeeds Jamie Hazard in the CFO role following Hazard’s transition to Chief Executive Officer in December of 2025. In her role as CFO, Coppenrath will oversee financial strategy, planning and operations for one of the largest community health centers in the country, with more than $300M in annual revenue, 1700 employees, and a full complement of primary care and specialty services, a 24/7 emergency department, the Neighborhood PACE program, and multiple on-site pharmacies.

“Maria stood out throughout the search process for both her exceptional financial leadership and her deep commitment to community health,” said Jamie Hazard, President and CEO of NeighborHealth. “She brings a rare combination of strategic expertise, operational excellence, and mission alignment. We are thrilled to welcome her to NeighborHealth and are confident she will be an invaluable partner as we continue to strengthen and expand care for the communities we serve.”

Born and raised in Everett, Massachusetts, Coppenrath distinguishes herself through her collaborative leadership style, ability to translate complex financial concepts into actionable insights, and proven track record of building high-performing finance teams.

“NeighborHealth is well known for its innovation, compassion, and service, and I am honored to be part of an organization so deeply committed to equitable, community-based care,” said Coppenrath. “As a first-generation Italian American and the daughter of immigrants, I understand the hopes, sacrifices, and resilience that shape so many of the families NeighborHealth serves. That personal connection makes it especially meaningful to join an organization that has long been a trusted resource for immigrant communities. I look forward to partnering with the leadership team and staff to support NeighborHealth’s continued growth, strengthen its mission, and ensure its long-term sustainability.”

Coppenrath joins NeighborHealth following a highly competitive national search. She is the recipient of numerous professional recognitions, including the National Association of Community Health Centers’ Jeffrey T. Latman Leadership in Healthcare Finance Award, and serves as Treasurer on the Board of Directors of the Massachusetts League of Community Health Centers. She is also on the Board of Directors of CapitalLink, which provides financial advisory services to community health centers in Massachusetts.

About NeighborHealth

NeighborHealth, formerly East Boston Neighborhood Health Center, is one of the nation’s largest Federally Qualified Health Centers (FQHCs) and the largest community-based primary care health system in Massachusetts, serving more than 120,000 people and recognized by the Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) as a National Quality Leader. For more than 50 years, NeighborHealth has offered access to comprehensive primary care, specialty care and emergency care for patients in the Commonwealth. In a first-of-its-kind merger in 2020, East Boston Neighborhood Health Center joined forces with the South End Community Health Center and expanded services to patients who live and work in Boston’s South End and Roxbury neighborhoods. NeighborHealth champions innovative care models to actively bridge health equity gaps in our low-income and immigrant communities with services unique from other healthcare providers in our state. For more information, visit www.neighborhealth.com.

###

The post NeighborHealth Appoints Maria Coppenrath as Chief Financial Officer first appeared on EastBoston.com.

news_communities.title: Sal Baglio’s new record is now out!
news_communities.author: admin
news_communities.summary:
Update: The Amplifier Heads stir up a rock and roll frenzy through a ‘Super 8’ lens: Sal Baglio delivers a nostalgic ode to garage rock and power-pop on a new album out on CD and digital via Rum Bar Records as of today, Friday, July 17

BOSTON, Mass. [June 10, 2026] — Sal Baglio of The Amplifier Heads knows a thing or two about the power of rock and roll.

Nearly 50 years ago, the East Boston native was front and center of a riot down at Boston University. It was spring of ‘82, and his band The Stompers were tearing through a live set at a campus block party when the noise complaints started flooding in – and soon, too, did the police.

The cops showed up in the middle of the Stompers’ final tune, killed the music, and all hell broke loose among the 700 or so in attendance. A hundred police officers rolled in with attack dogs, clubs, and mace; the sound man was arrested, charged with inciting a riot; the drummer was handcuffed; and the rest of the band were quickly whisked away by management. More than 50 attendees were hauled off to the clink.

The events of that day are just a blip on the rock and roll history timeline, but they belonged to Baglio and the band. Now the Boston music scene veteran takes a panoramic look at the power that’s shaped his sound and soul with the brash and bold Super 8, The Amplifier Heads’ new album set for release on compact disc and digital come Friday, July 17 on Rum Bar Records.

From the iconic and cultural kickstarting Moondog Coronation Ball in Cleveland in 1952, which inspires the barnstorming opening track and new single “Rock N Roll Riot” – streaming on Wednesday, June 24 – to all the highs and lows in the decades that followed, beaming from live stages across the country and gleaming off the radio dial across airwaves heard around the world, Baglio harnesses the power of rock and roll into a 30-minute soundtrack to his most enduring love.

And as Baglio takes us on a rock and roll history ride, all the bumps and bruises are part of the story, a slice of loud and raucous American life that hasn’t been the same since the youth began swinging guitars around.

“Super 8 is a grainy film,” Baglio says. “Magic fingers in a cheap hotel. A skip and scratch of a 45 on an old hot RCA Victor. Memory. Lotsa sax. The sound of an AM radio just off the dial on top of a kitchen table 1965. An old Circus magazine in a basement box. The banging of an old soda machine outside of that cheap hotel at 4 a.m. Ridin’ in a car, windows down and singing loud with your friends that summer. And it’s a rock n’ roll record by The Amplifier Heads.”

The Amplifier Heads’ follow-up to 2022 album Rectifier and the 2024 soundtrack to They Came To Rock, Norty Cohen’s sci-fi rock opera about aliens coming to Earth after receiving a radio signal sent by electric guitar, the garage rock and power-pop styling of Super 8 finds Baglio in familiar territory. And it’s not unlike what we heard in Pop Rocks in my Chewing Gum, the beloved 2025 album from his collaborative project with Dan Kopko, The Peppermint Kicks.

Over eight songs, as well as a digital bonus track in the groove-laden “Deuce Machine,” Baglio and his head full of rock and roll nostalgia takes us on a trip that’s both moving backwards and forward at the same time. It connects rock and roll history like an aural loop, where the events of 75 years ago not only feel like yesterday, suddenly feel like the future.

The sonic potency of the past is matched by the songwriter’s uncanny knack for timeless rock and roll bangers that draw from all that came before it. And because history matters in the game of rock and roll, crafting an album in the age of the single was a necessary endeavor – modern-day attention spans and economic uncertainty be damned.

“I had to work fast n’ cheap,” Baglio admits. “It went by like a Bukowski tremor. Sold all my Moonglows and Five Satins vinyl, and pawned a brownface Champ amp. Got a couple hours sleep in unmade rooms of cheap motels then up and out early before I got caught. Drank Sanka in the mornin’ out of Mad Dog cups from the night before. Ya know, can’t stop the rock…”

Baglio tends to write in gigantic, manic spurts, and the songs here on Super 8 – from the yearning garage rock strut of “Can’t Put My Finger On It” to the cool casual guitar-pop glide of They Don’t Dance Anymore” to the hip-shakin’ and body surfin’ tones of “A Girl Named Chop Suey” – all came together in one inspired stretch of conception.

A tone was set in May with the release of lead single “A Song Called Sha La La,” a driving arena-ready cruiser that aims for the rock and roll heavens and never holds back. It’s got a transportive quality to it, a balanced feeling of familiarity and freshness, and Baglio says “its intent is to make people sing LOUD, squeezing joy out from under misery.”

Finding joy in joyless times, that’s a common theme across the album. So Baglio is looking back to keep moving forward. What emerges through Super 8 are familiar storylines and historical anecdotes, starting with the rock and roll riot craze of the ‘50s, swirling around a midnight daydream where big loud drums, restless guitars, and a whole lotta saxophone cradle a barrage of hooks and melodies. And there’s some abrasive distorted screaming thrown in for good measure, because hey, this record is coming out in 2026, after all.

Through its 30-minute runtime, a collective tissue emerges from the lens of one of Boston’s greatest songwriters, and while the song titles give away the themes – the cool street-view bluesy swagger of “Hot Wax” to the gritty British Invasion and glam of “Superstar” to a ‘70s throwback groove of “Hot Stars” – there’s always something a little extra bubbling up from down under the surface.

“There will be certain groups of songs that get along well with one another and paint a particular picture,” Baglio admits. “Whether Jackson Pollack or Norman Rockwell, who really knows?!!? The songs sometimes ask for brothers, sisters, and friends to play with, which then sounds the signal alarm, a Little Richard scream, that it is more music procreation time!”

With Baglio writing the music and lyrics and contributing vocals, guitars, piano, organ, glockenspiel, and some hot bass on “Hot Wax,” Super 8 features The Amplifier Heads’ rhythm section that appears on most of the band’s records, in Kevin “King” Rapillo on drums and Brad Hallen on bass.

The sound here is augmented by The Jambalaya Horns, featuring Carlos Menenzes Jr., Matthew Naeger, and Henley Douglas Jr., while the dependable Jeff “G-Man” Giacomelli added sax solos to “A Song Called Sha La La” and “Hot Wax.”

Super 8 has a super collection of collaborators.

Recorded at Bang-A-Song Studios in Gloucester, Massachusetts, Super 8 was produced by Baglio, mixed by Warren Babson, and mastered by Alex McCollough at True East Mastering in Nashville, Tennessee. The record is dedicated to a pair of players in the Boston scene who have recently passed, power pop legend Paul Armstrong of The Flashcubes and beloved Grammy-winning engineer, producer, and studio wizard Ducky Carlisle.

The artwork was created by Glenn Robinson Design, capturing vivid scenes of pop culture history, each image a portal to a lost world where all that remains is the sound and the memory. Because Super 8 is a time capsule, a flash bang, and a sonic trip back to the past to help make sense of the present.

Baglio remembers the time he found himself in the middle of a riot, but his greatest memories of rock and roll didn’t come from him, they came to him. From the Moondog Ball back in ‘52, to that killer local show this past weekend at the neighborhood dive, rock and roll is the connective tissue through the times when we all felt truly alive

Baglio has been hooked from the start. And he’s got one lasting piece of advice as we welcome Super 8 into our lives.

“Find a jukebox,” he concludes, “stack a record player, turn it up loud, sing sha la la, dance again, jump up, spin around, get dizzy. Stomp n’ shout, laugh, go crazy, kick up some dirt, turn that amp up to 11. Blow yer speakers and start a rock n roll riot!”

He should know. He was there. And so were you.

Originally posted on June 10, 2026

The post Sal Baglio’s new record is now out! first appeared on EastBoston.com.

news_communities.title: Selectmen support audit of state Legislature
news_communities.author: Jim Mitchell
news_communities.summary:
At Tuesday night’s meeting, the Saugus Board of Selectmen voted unanimously to send a letter to state legislative leaders expressing support for an audit of the state Legislature. “The Saugus Board of Selectmen writes to express strong support for implementing Question 1 from the November 2024 ballot, Board of Selectmen Chair Debra Panetta wrote in a letter addressed to Speaker of the House Ronald Mariano and President of the Senate Karen E. Spilka.

“The Saugus voters approved Question 1 by 71.9 percent, joining with 72 percent of voters statewide, authorizing State Auditor Diana DiZoglio to audit the Massachusetts Legislature, the letter continued.

“The Legislature remains the only state entity that does not permit oversight by the State Auditor’s Office. While legislative leaders have claimed that internal audits conducted by a privately procured vendor are sufficient, the voters of Massachusetts have clearly expressed their desire for independent review. At a time when the Massachusetts Legislature ranks among the least transparent in the nation and is one of only four state legislatures exempt from public records laws, independent auditing is essential.

“Government entities that self-regulate pose risks of self interest and reduced accountability to voters. Implementing this audit would enhance government transparency, accountability, and fiscal responsibility. The Massachusetts Legislature shouldn’t be exempt from independent oversight, especially regarding the use of taxpayer funds.

“We urge you to take the necessary steps to honor the will of the voters and allow the State Auditor’s Office to proceed with this audit.”

Selectman Michael Serino noted that all 351 cities and towns are required to do a yearly audit. “I don’t see why the state Legislature should be exempt. They should do a yearly audit, too, to see that taxpayers’ money is spent appropriately,” Serino said.

He also noted that the board has a good working relationship with the Saugus legislative delegation. “This is not about them in particular. This is about conducting yearly financial audits. Making sure the taxpayers’ dollars are appropriated and spent on issues that are appropriate and the bidding process is followed.”

news_communities.title: No More Fees For Free
news_communities.author: Jim Mitchell
news_communities.summary:
Selectmen vote to establish several new fees for work done by their clerk on licenses and permits

 

By Mark E. Vogler

 

For years, the town has charged a mere $100 fee for the paperwork involved in preparing an application for a Special Permit (S-2). That was a bargain for the applicants, as the Clerk for the Board of Selectmen would spend an estimated nine to 15 hours working on applications for S-2 permits. Selectmen voted unanimously at Tuesday night’s (July 14) meeting to double the fee to $200.

The town had previously never charged a fee for the transfer of a liquor license – a task that could take 22 to 30 hours to complete. Selectmen voted to establish a new $350 fee for that transaction instead of doing it for free. Selectmen also voted to amend the Licensing Fee Schedule by establishing an application and a $100 fee for Bring Your Own Bottle (BYOB).

Board of Selectmen Chair Debra Panetta noted that July 15, 2003 – 23 years ago – was the last time that Saugus adjusted its fees.

news_communities.title: Saugus Gardens in the Summer
news_communities.author: Jim Mitchell
news_communities.summary:
Here’s what’s blooming in town this week to make your walks more enjoyable

 

By Laura Eisener

 

This past weekend I was delighted to see the first monarch butterflies to visit my garden this summer. Their arrival often coincides with the first blossoms on the butterfly bush (Buddleia davidii), which is one of their favorite nectar sources. This fragrant shrub, sometimes called summer lilac because of its fragrance although it is not closely related to lilac, attracts a wide range of other pollinators as well.

According to the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service, Monarch butterflies (Danaus plexippus) fall into two distinct populations: the range of eastern monarchs is between Canada and Mexico, travelling through most states east of the Rocky Mountains, while the western population stays west of the Rockies and goes from Canada to California and the southern United States, with many overwintering in southern California. There are some in Florida which don’t seem to migrate nearly as far, if at all, as others in their species. The longest migrations, about 3,000 miles, may take more than one generation. Western populations seem to be in more severe decline than eastern ones, and habitat loss may be one of the reasons.

Gardeners can help monarchs in general by planting more of their host plants, which are several non-tropical species of milkweeds, including butterfly weed (Asclepias tuberosa), swamp milkweed (Asclepias incarnata) and common milkweed (Asclepias syriaca). Just a few human generations ago, common milkweed was generally considered a weed to be eliminated in a well-cared for garden. Gradually people began to appreciate this plant’s importance for the monarch’s reproduction, and more people are planting this native plant with its spherical umbels of fragrant pinkish purple flowers instead of removing it from their gardens.

All members of the milkweed genus have sticky white sap that is toxic to many pests and predators so helps protect the plant. This toxicity is passed on to the monarch butterfly’s larvae, who are not harmed by it, but in turn it protects them from predators who might prey on them. Both the larvae and the adult butterfly are often avoided by insects and birds that regularly eat other butterflies. In turn, some look-alike orange butterflies may benefit from their similarity or mimicry of the monarch’s appearance. The viceroy butterfly (Limenitis archippus) is not toxic, but many predators may avoid it because of its similarity to the monarch butterfly. The milkweed plants, their sap and the butterflies are not toxic to the touch, but only if eaten. The sap can present challenges for anyone wanting to use the milkweed flowers in bouquets, as the sticky sap can prevent other flowers in the arrangement from taking up water

In addition to the select species that the monarch butterflies can lay eggs on, they need nectar from a variety of flowers along their migration. Adult butterflies eat nectar from a wide range of flowers, native and introduced, and occasionally also pollen and even fermented fruits. They can also eat the nectar from the milkweeds, but they need other flowers during the spring and fall outside the milkweed blooming period. Joe Pye weed (Eutrochium spp.), New England asters (Symphiotrichum novae-angliae) and several goldenrods (Solidago spp.) help give the butterflies energy for their fall migration.

news_communities.title: Remembering Saugus Town Manager Norman Bjarne Hansen
news_communities.author: Jim Mitchell
news_communities.summary:
By Janice K. Jarosz

 

  From the Bible: Luke 6:45: “A good person produces good things from the treasury of a good heart. A good man does not wait to be asked. He leads in decency, kindness, and generosity. He pursues what is right and does it gladly, with an open hand and a compassionate heart.”

 

Many terms could be used to describe Norman Hansen — dedicated family man, a man of faith, proud Marine, hardworking public servant and accomplished town manager with a priceless sense of humor. These terms are all the more remarkable because he grew up as a poor orphan during the Great Depression. He overcame a tough lot in life and forged a career as one of the town’s most respected citizens.

Norman Bjarne Hansen was born in Berlin, New Hampshire, on July 8, 1924, to the late Bjarne and Dagne (Johnson) Hansen but sadly his mother passed away when he was six years old. Two paternal aunts took over raising him throughout his young life and he cherished both of them. Norman loved the outdoors and excelled at both football and hockey in his high school years.

Shortly after graduating from high school, he enlisted in the U.S. Marines Corps during World War II. He served with the 4th division stationed in Maui, where he met and shared quarters with Thomas McGee, former Speaker of the House. Together, they were shipped out to conflicts in the Marshall Islands, Saipan, Tinian and Iwo Jima. During his service he survived some of the bloodiest campaigns in the war, including the Battle of Iwo Jima.

Once discharged in 1945, Norman moved to Lynn to live with his brother and enrolled in Burdett Business College. According to a well-known informant, Norm, who could pass for the movie star Errol Flynn, spied Evelyn Pratt as she was getting ready to leave a dance they both attended. He ran after her and asked for her telephone number, which she gladly gave to him. That chance meeting lit the spark of a love story that lasted a lifetime.

The wedding was held at her parents’ home on Easter Sunday with over 100 attendees, and for the next 20 years, Norm was employed at the General Electric Company as an accountant. He was also a talented carpenter and in 1946, the couple purchased property on Adams Avenue Norm physically built their home with his own sweat, hands and tools.

No doubt, the friendship with his now Democratic father-in-law County Commissioner C.F. Nelson Pratt eventually drew him into the hot political bed of Saugus, and when the present town accountant became ill and resigned, Norm saw his chance, took the Civil Service Exam and was appointed town accountant.

His political affiliations and volunteer contributions to the town down through the years were extensive. He served as a member of the Finance Committee, temporary town manager from 1973-74, 1977-78 and 1978-81, coached Little League baseball, was one of the founders of Saugus Youth Hockey, member of the VFW Post 2346, American Legion Post 210 and the DAV, all while he and his wife were “keeping house” and raising five children. When elected Selectman, he was voted in as chairman, 5-0.

Due to his close relationships with many Saugonians, he became known and respected throughout the town for his financial acumen, and in the late 80’s he was appointed full-time town manager from 1987-1991.

Saugonian Newspaper Reporter Madeline Marino interviewed several members of the Selectmen while Mr. Hansen was town manager. Selectman Bob Long recalled an incident: “During a very heated discussion, the town manager turned to us and rather bluntly asked, ‘Ok, you five ran for office, now tell me what you want me to do.’”

Selectman Long continued, “Town Manager Hansen was the last Saugus manager who truly believed in the Charter as written; the manager runs the municipal operations on a daily basis and reports to the selectmen. Norm did not expect us to be (his words) “just pretty faces showing up for business ribbon cuttings.” Selectman Long continued: “If an important issue came up, the town manager called all of us one at a time. He is a terrific person; a ‘rock solid’ town manager and I think the world of him.”

During his tenure as town manager, the selectmen’s office and the town manager’s office were side by side with doors open to the public most of the time. A second door was shared between the town manager and the board in between both offices in the back of the rooms. Offices were open to the public; benches lined both sides of the hall for public convenience; and oftentimes, Norm would wander out to the lobby, greeting residents, and engage in conversations.

Ms. Marino also interviewed Chairman of the BOS Janette Fasano: “I credit him for treating taxpayer dollars as if they had come from his own pockets. He cared about our town; Saugus was his second family.”

Another interview with former Fire Chief James Blanchard: “I grew up several doors down from the Hansen family. Mr. Hansen came from a hard-scrabble family background, pulled himself up by the bootstraps and went on to be very successful in all aspects of his life. He never forgot where he came from; he was a distinguished role model for all of us.”

Ms. Marino also met with both Mr. and Mrs. Hansen, and the couple shared many thoughts on their lives together. “We each had separate roles as parents,” Evelyn explained. “I tended to the needs of our family and others, and we had to share a car! I was raised in a political family and familiar with the needs and duties of a public official. I did my job, and Norm did his. We worked as a team.”

The reporter commented to me how she saw firsthand the love, respect and humor each partner shared. When she asked their ages, Norm proudly replied, “I am 79 7/12th and Evelyn is 77 19/24th.”

Though Evelyn looked a little pained, she did chuckle, “Keep in mind Madelene, he’s an accountant!”

Norm retired in 1991 and neither his staff nor the public would let him go without a proper sendoff. Accountants Betty Quinlan and Gail Peach and others booked the Caruso Diplomat on Route One to host the event. Tickets were sold out in three days, and the Diplomat was filled with over 400 well-wishers.

A parade formed from the back of the hall and was led by Public Works Director Joe Attubato, followed by Randy Briand carrying the American Flag. Then came the Saugus High School Band, Police and Fire Chiefs, Town and School officials, Clergy, Marines, bank presidents, Boy Scouts, Girl Scouts, Sports figures — all marching in to “America the Beautiful” — everyone was standing, clapping, and some with tears welling up in their eyes, paying a most deserved tribute to a great and beloved man as the entire parade passed by the head table honoring the Hansen family. Those shared farewell moments will never be forgotten.

Norman and Evelyn were married for 62 years; Evelyn passed on May 6, 2009, and Norman passed on October 27, 2014, at 90 years of age. Town Manger Norman Hansen was a man of faith, of courage and respect for everyone; he was one of the most beloved men to ever walk amongst us.

news_communities.title: Wally coming to World Series Park
news_communities.author: Jim Mitchell
news_communities.summary:
Fenway favorite will lead a cast of costumed characters to town for Saugus Olympics Day celebration on Sept. 26

 

  (The following article is based on a recent press release issued by World Series Park.)

 

Wally, the Boston Red Sox mascot, will visit Saugus Olympics Day at World Series Park in Saugus on Saturday, Sept. 26, from 9 a.m. to 4 p.m. Appearances by costumed characters Fisher Cat, Canaligator, Blades and Sea Dog will also be part of the festivities. For nine years World Series Park has hosted a Special Olympics Day, raising $150,000 for Special Olympics.

“This type of event has great meaning and satisfaction, so we wanted to bring it back this year with different non-profit organizations,” Superintendent of World Series Park Bob Davis said.

“Challenger Baseball and Boys & Girls Club are those organizations that will take part and be the recipients of this fundraiser,” he said.

A baseball tournament involving Challenger Baseball teams from the area will start at 9 a.m. Entertainment will start on the stage at 10 a.m. and will be provided by the Saugus High Chorus, the Memorylaners featuring Howie “Sachmo” Conley, Beat ConnXtionz dancers, Just the Three of Us, the Rosa Singers and Joey B-Bop. At 11 a.m. the opening ceremony and parade of athletes will take place, emceed by Boston Celtics announcer Eddie Palladino. Gerry Agganis will throw out the first pitch.

Nancy Kerrigan, a former Olympic medalist, will take part in the opening ceremony and will be available to meet and greet those in attendance. Other former Olympic medalists, including athletes who competed in this year’s Winter Olympics, have also been invited. The Olympic athletes will be honored during the opening ceremony.

The whole day will take on a festival atmosphere, including raffles, a moon-bounce, classic cars, booths, games, hayrides, costumed characters and a large variety of food and drink. The event is free and open to the public. The rain date will be the next day, Sunday, September 27.

World Series Park is proud to again sponsor this type of event with all proceeds going to Challenger Baseball and Boys & Girls Club. Donations from businesses and individuals can be made prior to that day and will be acknowledged at the event. A fishbowl will be available for donations at the event. All checks should be made payable to World Series Park, 8 Holden Ave., Saugus, MA 01906.

Anyone interested and willing to help with this event can contact Bob Davis at 781-307-6239.

news_communities.title: Saugus Selectmen Salute Retiring Police Lieutenant
news_communities.author: Jim Mitchell
news_communities.summary:
Lt. Arthur Connors praised as “dedicated,” “helpful,” “phenomenal” and “amazing” liaison to the board

 

By Mark E. Vogler

 

As the Saugus Police Department’s longtime liaison to the Board of Selectmen, Police Lt. Arthur D. Connors, Jr., spent countless hours over the years collaborating with town officials to address citizen public safety concerns. Over the last decade, nobody in the Saugus Police Department made as many visits to the second floor auditorium to brief selectmen or answer their questions on various issues – many of them having to do with traffic; all of them having to do with making Saugus safer for its citizens.

Lt. Connors, who has also served for about a decade as a Town Meeting Member representing Precinct 3, attended his final meeting as the Police Department’s liaison on Tuesday night (July 14) – just days before he retired from the police force after 23 years on the job. Instead of briefing selectmen or answering their questions, he stood at the speaker’s lectern, facing them as they said their farewells and thanked him for his service. They also presented Lt. Connors with a framed citation, commending him for his service.

“It’s given to somebody that everybody on the board can call their friend.

“I am honored this evening to present Lt. Art Connors with the citation, to recognize all his hard work, helpfulness and dedication to the Town of Saugus,” Board of Selectmen Chair Debra Panetta said.

“Lt. Connors has been a selectmen liaison for many, many years. And we appreciate everything he has done, not just to help this board, but to help all of our residents,” she said.

The citation read as follows: “To all persons whom these presents shall come. Be it known that on this day, July 14, 2026, the Town of Saugus recognizes Lieutenant Arthur Connors for his 23 years of exceptional dedicated service to the Town of Saugus in law enforcement during which time he demonstrated unwavering commitment to protecting and serving the community. Congratulations Lieutenant Connors on a well-earned retirement and continued health, happiness and success in the years ahead.”

All five selectmen signed the citation. Here’s what each selectman and the town manager had to say to Lt. Connors:

Selectman Michael Serino: “Lt. Connors, I want to thank you for your service to our community, not only the help that you provided this board all these years. You’re part of our little family here, and we’re certainly going to miss you and want to wish you all of the best, you and your family. Thank you.”

Selectman Frank Federico: “Lt. Connors, I just want to congratulate you on your years of service to the community of Saugus. My time on the board with you has been short so far. But prior to that, we were on the Town Meeting together. You’ve always been more than generous as far as answering questions and explaining many issues that may have come up. I know you had a countdown on your phone and you were looking forward to this. So, I wish you nothing but the best in retirement and hope you can enjoy it to the fullest. And it’s very well deserved.”

Selectman Jeff Cicolini: “So Lt. Connors, it has been an absolute pleasure. I’ve served with several officers in your capacity. You have been the utmost professional. You’ve dealt with some difficult situations and some not so pleasant situations. More importantly, I want to thank you for being the person you are and the professional you are and for all you have done – not just in this capacity – but to keep our town safe. I tell everybody we are lucky to have the best of the best in both our police and fire, and I stand by those words. And you are the epitome of a professional, and I’m honored to call you my friend. I wish you the best retirement. Well deserved. And I know you are going to continue to enjoy yourself and have fun. It’s so well-deserved, my friend. Thank you again for everything. You’ve been amazing to work with.”

Board of Selectmen Vice Chair Anthony Cogliano: “If there’s a person in town that no one can say a bad word about, it’s certainly you. I value your friendship and all you have done for the community over the years, and you will surely be missed. Thank you very much, my friend.”

Board of Selectmen Chair Debra Panetta: “And I echo everything that my fellow board members have said. You have just been phenomenal over the years. Every time there’s an issue, you’re right there to help us out, help the residents out. We couldn’t have asked for a better, more helpful and dedicated liaison. You’ve been absolutely wonderful.”

Town Manager Scott C. Crabtree: “Lt. Connors, I just wanted to congratulate you on retiring and for a long and great career. And I wish you nice peace and relaxation. On behalf of the town and myself, thank you very much for all of your service to this community.”

After selectmen and the town manager had completed their remarks, Panetta asked if Lt. Connors would like to say a few words. “Absolutely, not,” Lt. Connors responded, which drew laughter from the board and audience.
