news_communities.title: Parishioners devastated to learn Mass. priest must leave the U.S. over his visa
news_communities.author: Mary Markos
news_communities.summary:
Parishioners at a Massachusetts church say they are devastated — and worried — after learning their priest is being forced to leave the country due to immigration issues.

The emotional announcement came during Mass on Sunday, catching many in the congregation off guard.

“You could see the look of fear in this man’s eyes,” said Arlene Lesch, a longtime member of Holy Family Parish in Gloucester.

Lesch says the priest, who parishioners know as “Father Ben,” told the congregation he is being sent back to Nigeria because of immigration challenges.

Community still processing sudden departure

Members of Holy Family Parish are now trying to process how someone they’ve seen every week for the past four years could suddenly be forced to leave.

“I’m just really worried for Father Ben,” said parishioner Nicole Altieri. “I know that it’s not safe in Nigeria for Catholic priests right now.”

NBC10 Boston is not identifying or showing the priest due to privacy concerns, but parishioners describe him as a constant presence in their community.

Lesch, who has been part of the church for nearly 40 years, says the news has shaken the congregation.

“It just broke everybody’s heart watching this and experiencing it,” she said.

A leader in the church and the community

According to church leaders, the priest has been serving at St. Ann’s and Our Lady of Good Voyage in Gloucester as well as St. Joachim’s in Rockport while also working as a chaplain at Salem Hospital, supporting patients and families during some of their most difficult moments.

“I just have not been able to think of anything since because he’s been such an asset,” Lesch added.

Local leaders say his departure would come at a time when there is already a shortage of clergy.

“There’s a shortage of priests not just in the Archdiocese of Boston, but also across the country,” said Paul Murphy, chairman of the Rockport Select Board.

Visa expiring, uncertainty ahead

The Archdiocese of Boston confirms the priest’s visa is set to expire next month, and there is currently no path to extend it under existing U.S. policy.

Murphy says the uncertainty surrounding the situation is adding to the community’s concern.

“It’s heartbreaking to know he may be in harm’s way. We don’t know the whole situation. That’s adding to the angst,” he said. “Hopefully the Archdiocese will come out with a statement.”

Calls for action

NBC10 Boston reached out to several members of Massachusetts’ congressional delegation, including Sens. Elizabeth Warren and Ed Markey and Congressman Seth Moulton, to ask whether anything can be done at the federal level, but has not yet heard back.

news_communities.title: 17 statues around Boston that got the Scottish traffic cone treatment this summer
news_communities.author: Asher Klein
news_communities.summary:
Scotland‘s Tartan Army has left an indelible mark on the city of Boston. One part of the fan group’s legacy: placing traffic cones on the heads of statues around town.

It’s a tradition dating back about four decades in Glasgow, where the equestrian statue of the Duke of Wellington is regularly crowned with a traffic cone. Here’s how the city described the legend and its significance in a bid to host the headquarters of the TV company Channel 4:

At the height of a bacchanalian night an unknown reveller, possibly a student, defied gravity to clamber up a 21-foot monument, and place a stolen traffic cone on the head of the Duke of Wellington. No one knows the identity of the reveller or how they managed to get up on to the statue, but it happened on an intoxicating night, when Glasgow was about to be crowned European Capital of Culture. … And so a 19th Century military hero with a traffic cone on his head has come to personify the city. This is the Glasgow we want to present – a city alive with attitude, teeming with young people and ignited with an audacious belief in its own creativity.

With the fan base headed to Miami for Scotland’s last World Cup group game, it remains to be seen if the traffic cones will stay on the heads of statues around the Boston area, but the images will be a long-lasting reminder of the city’s Scottish summer.

Here are many of the statues that were fitted with new, orange hats in and around Boston during the World Cup:

Samuel Adams

The Duckling statues

Salem’s “Bewitched” statue

Bill Russell

Mayor Kevin White

Parkman Plaza’s tributes to industry, religion and learning

Edward Everett Hale

Did we miss any? Send your cone-adorned statue pictures to shareit@nbcboston.com.

news_communities.title: Dog found dead after being reported stolen on Saugus trail
news_communities.author: Jericho Tran
news_communities.summary:
Police say a dog was found dead after being reported stolen Wednesday morning on the North Shore of Massachusetts.

Sophie Ellis-Stetsy said her French bulldog mix, Mama Maisie, was stolen while she was walking on a trail off Route 1 near the Saugus-Lynnfield line.

Saugus police told NBC10 Boston Friday that the dog had been found dead, noting that the investigation is ongoing.

Ellis-Stetsy, a dog walker, told NBC10 Boston she had the pet off-leash with several other dogs when a man walked up, picked up the dog and ran away.

“I’ve been doing this for 15 years, and this has never happened before,” she said.

Ellis-Stetsy said the man approached to talk to her.

“I saw a guy, kind of in the distance, and then he goes, ‘Nice dogs, are they friendly?’ and I said, ‘Yes,'” she said. “Then he just picked her up and then he sprinted off and I sprinted after him.”

She said she ran after him for 20 minutes, but could not catch up to him.

She had set out on the trail with several other dogs Wednesday morning as part of a dog walking company that she runs with her husband.

“We hike and train dogs off-leash, so they can run freely together on trails,” she said.

Maisie, who was 2 years old, was her foster dog and held a special place in the hearts of her owners.

“We’ll give you any amount of money that you want, just please bring her back safely,” she said Wednesday. “She’s not worth anything monetarily to me, she’s worth so much more.”

The owners offered a $5,000 reward for Maisie’s safe return.

news_communities.title: Man sentenced to decades in prison for deadly wrong-way Saugus crash
news_communities.author: Mike Pescaro
news_communities.summary:
A Massachusetts man has been sentenced to at least 24 years in prison for a Saugus crash that left a teenager dead.

The crash on June 9, 2021, killed 19-year-old Ashley Forward of Lynn. William Leger, 40, of Salem, received his sentence five years later to the day.

The Essex County District Attorney’s Office said that Leger fled from police after robbing a 7-Eleven store on Lincoln Avenue. He went south on Route 107 northbound and hit Forward’s vehicle.

Forward died at the scene, and Leger was hospitalized with non-life-threatening injuries. No one else was hurt.

Leger pleaded guilty to manslaughter, unarmed robbery and receiving a stolen vehicle. A judge sentenced him to 18-20 years in prison for the manslaughter, another six to eight years for unarmed robbery and five years’ probation after his release.

“Ashley was a remarkable young woman with a bright future ahead of her,” Forward’s mother, Michelle Luongo, said in a statement shared by prosecutors. “She was kind, compassionate, and always willing to help others no matter what … Nursing was not just a career choice for her — it reflected who she was. She wanted to care for people, to comfort them, and to heal them. Her life mattered deeply — not just to her family, her friends, or the community where she gave so much of her time, but to the people she would have helped along the way.”

More on the death of Ashley Forward

news_communities.title: Truck kills cyclist at busy Lynn intersection
news_communities.author: Asher Klein
news_communities.summary:
A person died after being hit by a truck in Lynn, Massachusetts, on Friday morning, police said.

The Essex County District Attorney’s Office said the victim, a male cyclist, was hit around 11 a.m. at the intersection of Lynnway and Commercial Street.

The man’s identity is being withheld until family can be notified.

Lynnway was closed except for left turns, Massachusetts State Police said after the crash. Lynn police said Commercial Street was closed during the state police investigation, urging drivers to avoid the area or expect delays. Police were seen directing traffic away from the site of the crash.

Police haven’t shared what they believe led to the tractor-trailer hitting the pedestrian, or if charges are expected to be filed. Prosecutors said the driver remained at the scene and is cooperating.

The cab of a tractor-trailer had been raised off the ground at the scene; a bicycle was seen on the ground nearby.

The cause of the crash is under investigation.

news_communities.title: Teen is identified decades after her grisly murder at Mass. YMCA
news_communities.author: Amelia Fabiano, Asher Klein and Marc Fortier
news_communities.summary:
A teenager whose name was unknown for over two decades following her murder at a Lynn, Massachusetts, YMCA has been identified.

The man who killed Tiffany Bradley and dumped part of her body at the parking lot of the Soldiers’ Home at Chelsea in November 2000 was convicted, but he didn’t know the 16-year-old’s name, officials said at a news conference in Boston where she was identified, and which family members attended.

“It is totally amazing that after 26 years, people care enough to giver her a name and return her to her family,” said Bradley’s niece, Shakirah Wiggins.

Until she was identified, Bradley had been known as “Chelsea Jane Doe.” A composite sketch of what she might have looked like was created after her head and hands were found in a bag buried at Nahant Beach, Suffolk County District Attorney Kevin Hayden said.

An FBI cold case team collected a DNA sample that led to her brother. Bradley had been reported missing on Nov. 8, 2000, from Allentown, Hayden said. She was killed days later, after being trafficked to Boston.

The top FBI agent in Boston, Special Agent in Charge Ted Docks, said Bradley had been strangled, decapitated and dismembered, and the only hint investigators had from confessed killer Eugene McCollom was that her name might have been Lisa.

“Identifying victims of violent crime matters,” Docks said. “It matters even when the offender’s already been caught. It matters even when the case has already been solved.”

The head of the Massachusetts State Police, Col. Geoffrey Noble, noted that this is a rare case where a suspect is identified before the victim is.

An emotional Bradley’s aunt, Janet Bradley-Knight, offered her deep thanks to investigators for “not letting my baby be a box on a shelf.” She described Bradley as a joyful child, who loved to dance, draw and play basketball.

McCollom remains in prison but is soon eligible for parole. Bradley’s family wanted him to know, as Wiggins put it, “We pray that you are freed from the demons torturing you so that you too can experience peace.”

Officials initially said that Bradley was 17, but later clarified that she would have turned 17 just days after her body was found on Nov. 13.

news_communities.title: Teen critically hurt in daylight stabbing at Lynn playground
news_communities.author: Asher Klein
news_communities.summary:
A teenager was stabbed at a playground in Lynn, Massachusetts, on Friday afternoon, police said.

The 16-year-old was rushed to a hospital from the Bennett Street Playground, at Elmwood Avenue, after a report of assault and battery at 1:32 p.m., according to Lynn police. He was in critical but stable condition.

There was believed to be no danger to the public, police said, calling it an isolated incident.

They didn’t say if anyone was in custody or what they suspect led to the stabbing.

Parts of the playground and surrounding park were taped off amid the investigation on Friday afternoon.

news_communities.title: Driver flees police, hits cruiser and escapes after chase through Boston suburbs
news_communities.author: Marc Fortier and Asher Klein
news_communities.summary:
A trio of people fled a vehicle that led police on a chase through the Boston suburbs on Friday, damaging a Massachusetts State Police cruiser in the process, officials said.

No arrests were announced by Friday night despite a large search involving helicopters and police dogs. Residents in Stoneham, where the vehicle was ditched, were shocked at the scene.

The incident began in Everett, state police said, when the vehicle fled a traffic stop. It sideswiped a state police cruiser in Malden before continuing on. The trooper inside the damaged vehicle didn’t have serious injuries.

Malden police said other law enforcement agencies were following a vehicle whose driver had multiple warrants out for their arrest. When the vehicle entered Malden, the other agencies radioed Malden police asking for their help.

Police responded to Main Street and Bickford Road, where they also tried to stop the vehicle. But the driver tried to drive away and hit an unmarked cruiser from another agency in the process. A uniformed Malden officer then got out of his cruiser to try to help and he was nearly struck by the driver’s vehicle trying to get away.

The vehicle was later found abandoned on Lindenwood Road in Stoneham The driver and passenger reportedly fled on foot.

Police said there was no threat to the general public. Police K-9 units could be seen searching the area where the vehicle was abandoned.

Everett police confirmed that they were involved in the pursuit, which was the result of a drug investigation. They said the individual was wanted on several outstanding warrants and also cut his ankle bracelet off. They said he took off when police approached him.

Stoneham police also confirmed their involvement in the investigation, saying they were notified by state police around 11:20 a.m. that a vehicle wanted in connection with an investigation out of Everett was located at Lindenwood Road. They said the vehicle was abandoned prior to police arrival.

Officers from Stoneham responded to the area to assist with the search. A state police K9 unit and helicopter were also deployed. So far, they said the driver and passenger have not been found.

news_communities.title: Pros and cons of battery storage sites: Local concerns, safety updates
news_communities.author: Mary Markos
news_communities.summary:
Battery storage facilities are rapidly expanding across Massachusetts as the state pushes toward cleaner, more reliable energy.

But as these projects move closer to neighborhoods, they’re also raising concerns about safety and community input.

What is battery storage, and why is it growing?

Battery energy storage systems are designed to store electricity and release it when demand is high, acting like a backup power source for the grid.

“That’s a really key technology for unlocking a lot of savings for customers,” said Massachusetts Undersecretary of Energy Mike Judge. “It helps reduce peak demand and avoid building new, costly infrastructure.”

The state has already deployed thousands of smaller battery systems. Now, developers are proposing larger, utility-scale projects — some capable of storing hundreds of megawatts of power — to support renewable energy sources like wind and solar.

A massive project in an industrial area

In Everett, one project by Jupiter Power is about to become the largest battery storage facility in Massachusetts, and possibly all of New England.

“This used to be an area dominated by fossil fuels,” said John Walkey, director of climate justice at GreenRoots. “Now we’re talking about clean energy that makes the grid more reliable and more affordable over time.”

The proposed facility will be split across two parcels in an industrial zone, one reason some residents see it as an appropriate location. Still, concerns remain — even in industrial areas.

“People do have concerns in terms of fires, explosions, things like that,” Walkey said.

Safety: What the data shows

Battery fires have made headlines here and in other parts of the country, raising questions about whether large-scale storage facilities are safe.

But researchers say the technology has improved significantly.

“We’ve calculated about a 99% reduction in failure rate over the last seven years,” said Lakshmi Srinivasan, a battery safety researcher with the Electric Power Research Institute.

Experts say that improvement is due to stronger safety standards, better system design and lessons learned from early incidents.

“There’s really an ecosystem of codes, standards and design practices,” Srinivasan said, “all focused on reducing both the likelihood and severity of failures.”

Why batteries matter for clean energy

Battery storage plays a critical role in the transition to renewable energy.

Solar panels only produce power during the day, and wind energy can fluctuate. Batteries store that energy so it can be used later — especially during peak demand, like hot summer evenings.

“When the sun’s not shining or the wind’s not blowing, that stored energy is there,” Walkey said.

Battery storage offers a cleaner alternative, reducing emissions and potentially lowering long-term energy costs.

Concerns in residential areas

While projects in industrial zones have seen comparatively less pushback, proposed battery facilities near residential neighborhoods are facing stronger opposition.

On the Saugus-Revere line, one project would place dozens of battery units, each roughly the size of a tractor-trailer, near homes and a local park.

“I have concerns about what’s going to happen here 15 or 20 years from now,” said Saugus resident and Town Meeting Member Robert Camuso.

More renewable energy news

Boston Business Journal Apr 21

Judge blocks Trump renewable energy freeze

2026 Milan Cortina Olympics Feb 21

Organizers of the Winter Games made clean energy a priority. Here's how they did it

Among his biggest worries: safety, long-term health impacts and lack of information.

“Not knowing the health impacts is a concern,” Camuso said. “If something tragic happens, it’s happening right here.”

Emergency response concerns

Battery fires differ significantly from typical structure fires, requiring specialized training and equipment.

“It’s not just dumping water on it,” Walkey explained. “There are chemical reactions, there are gases — and you need trained responders.”

Community input and environmental justice

Advocates say one of the biggest issues isn’t just safety, it’s process.

“Our biggest concern is making sure people are informed and have a voice,” Walkey said. “If something is coming into your neighborhood, you should be part of that conversation.”

Residents in Saugus say they want more transparency before projects move forward.

“I’d like to see more independent information,” Camuso said. “Something that tells us it’s safe before it’s built.”

What Happens Next

Many of these battery projects — including the one on the Revere-Saugus line — are still in early stages and require extensive local and state approvals.

That includes environmental reviews, fire safety planning and public input. Officials say the process can take years, and no final decisions have been made.

news_communities.title: Couple accused of racist assault at East Boston restaurant
news_communities.author: Thea DiGiammerino and Jericho Tran
news_communities.summary:
A couple from East Boston faces civil rights violations after allegedly attacking a group of people at a local restaurant, making threats and hurling racial slurs before turning to violence.

Arielle Raso, 37, was charged Tuesday with seven counts of assault and battery, seven counts of civil rights violation, seven counts of threatening to commit a crime, and one count of assault and battery with a dangerous weapon on a child under 14. Her husband Gregory Raso, 39, was charged with seven counts of assault and battery, seven counts of civil rights violation, and one count of assault and battery with a dangerous weapon.

The situation began around 6:45 p.m. Monday as a noise complaint, according to the Suffolk County District Attorney’s Office. The Rasos, who live next door to Tertulia’s Cafe on Porter Street, called police to make a noise complaint. Officers told the husband and wife to contact them if the noise continued past 11 p.m.

At 8:30 p.m., police were called back to the restaurant for a reported assault. The victim told officers that she and several others, who are Hispanic, were gathered at the restaurant when the Rasos began complaining about the noise. The situation escalated to a confrontation, with the Rasos allegedly telling the victims to “get out of this country,” using racial slurs, and threatening to kill the victims.

The victim said the Rasos broke off a piece of wood fencing and threw it at the group, hitting a child. They also sprayed the group with a hose.

According to the DA, an officer heard Arielle Raso admit to spraying the group with a hose and making threats.

“The assaults on this group were terrible alone, but the injection of such vile racial rhetoric makes this incident even more appalling. Our office will not—and our society must not—allow such actions to go unnoticed and unpunished,” District Attorney Kevin Hayden said in a media release.

The Rasos appeared in East Boston BMC on Tuesday. A judge set bail at $750 each and the couple was ordered to have no contact with the victims or other restaurant patrons. They are next scheduled to appear in court on July 28.

A family member of the couple declined to speak to reporters Wednesday but did say, without elaborating, that there are two sides to each story.

The owner of the restaurant, Gildardo Preciado, said the couple had been a continuous problem.

“They don’t like us, basically,” he said.

news_communities.title: The soccer show will go on in Boston as World Cup licenses are approved by FIFA
news_communities.author: Marc Fortier and Asher Klein
news_communities.summary:
Boston Mayor Michelle Wu said FIFA has granted the city’s licenses for the Boston Fan Fest and various other neighborhood watch parties planned during this summer’s World Cup, which will allow games to be shown there.

“We got our licenses,” she said during an appearance on GBH News’ “Boston Public Radio” on Wednesday. She said this includes the FIFA Fan Festival Boston activation planned for City Hall Plaza, as well as six different watch parties planned in various neighborhoods.

Her office announced later that the free watch parties will be held in Dorchester and East Boston and on the Boston Common, with further locations to be announced — see the full schedule below.

Wu was asked by host Jim Braude why the Fan Festival only runs from June 12-27, even though the World Cup runs through July 9.

“We will still have watch parties in different places, and we had some private funding and other resources to support neighborhood block parties,” she said. “But the security screening protocols and infrastructure is really intense to be able to host Fan Fest and accommodate large crowds… it does require shutting down access to a big part of City Hall Plaza for 24 hours even when the Fan Fest isn’t running, so there was a little bit of a tradeoff there. Did I wish we could have had a much longer Fan Fest and private resources and committee funding to do so? Yes. We had talked through that. This is what we were able to do.”

Ultimately, she said that was a decision made by the Boston Host Committee, Boston26.

Wu was also asked about the high cost of attending World Cup matches in Boston, including why a city like Philadelphia can offer cheaper train fares to their games and how New York Mayor Zohran Mamdani was able to make 1,000 tickets available for $50 apiece as part of a lottery.

“I think you know where I stand. I am always for trying to remove every barrier to public transit,” she said. “I know there are costs to offset, and the T is really ramping up capacity in a heroic way compared to what they are used to, their standard levels of service. I always believe we should make it as easy as possible for people to ride transit.”

Wu said ticket prices to the games are also set at this point, and she is not able to offer cheaper tickets like Mamdani.

More Massachusetts World Cup news

Boston May 27

Bill would push last call to 3 a.m. across Mass. for World Cup

World Cup May 27

Where do the USMNT's World Cup stars play professionally? Here are their clubs

2026 World Cup May 25

Former Melrose High School soccer player set to play in the World Cup, for Haiti

Part of the reason other cities were able to do this is that they were able to secure sponsorships to offset the costs or because they have sports hosting commissions as part of their permanent structure that bid for, host and have an entire fundraisign and resource generating infrastructure built up over time.

“Some of these cities are places we should learn from,” Wu said.

Asked if she is happy with the Boston26 organizing committee, the mayor offered a tepid endorsement.

“I think there are many people who have stepped up to take on really complicated work. Every major event is unique,” Wu said. “In this case, FIFA is its own unique entity to work with. Just getting licenses… you really have to be on their timeline, adn their requirements for security, it’s a lot to manage. I think Boston and Greater Boston and the Massachsuetts region, we know how to host big events. We will find a way no matter what the situation is. There’s been a lot of turnover there, a lot of fits and starts. It’s a lesson for me. As we look to host big events, we hae to have the underlying infrastructure. We have to really learn from this and be prepared and build permanent structures to handle this.”

That said, the mayor said she is expecting the World Cup to be a tremendous experience in Boston and across Massachusetts.

“I think we will have a great World Cup,” Wu said. “This is an incredible, generational opportunity for us… We will be busy all summer long. We’re really good also as a region of recognizing our shortcomings. We hold ourselves to high standards. We’re going to work as hard as we possibly can until we kick off, and afterward I think it’s also important to recognize what we did well and get back to pushing ourselves to be better next time.”

news_communities.title: Travel ban means many fans can't visit U.S. to watch Haiti in World Cup
news_communities.author: Oscar Margain
news_communities.summary:
Massachusetts leaders honored Haitian soccer star Frantzdy Pierrot on Tuesday as Haiti prepares for its first FIFA World Cup appearance in 52 years — but the celebration unfolded against the backdrop of an ongoing U.S. travel ban that is preventing fans in Haiti from attending the tournament.

Officials issued a state proclamation recognizing Pierrot, a member of Haiti’s national soccer team who grew up in Massachusetts. Gov. Maura Healey declared May 26 Frantzdy Pierrot Day.

The recognition came just weeks before the start of the 2026 FIFA World Cup, where Haiti was set to play Scotland at Boston Stadium on June 13.

The Trump administration’s travel ban on Haiti took effect nearly a year ago and expanded in January, restricting entry under most immigrant and nonimmigrant visas. The restrictions meant many Haitian fans likely would not be able to travel to the United States to support their national team in person.

Boston City Councilor-at-Large Ruthzee Louijeune, who is of Haitian descent, said Haitian officials tried to advocate for exceptions to the policy.

“There were efforts by the Haitian ambassador, there were efforts to ease up on the travel ban so that the Haitian fans that do exist in Haiti could come and travel to the game,” Louijeune said. “Not only is it bad for morale, but it’s bad for our economy.”

Immigration attorney Giselle Rodriguez said Haitian clients affected by the ban faced difficult realities as the World Cup approached.

Rodriguez said fans hoping to attend the tournament had little chance of securing visas under the current restrictions.

“It’s just extremely upsetting,” Rodriguez said. “If you’re applying for a visa now, as a spectator and a fan, I do not think there’s a chance for you.”

Haiti is not the only World Cup participant affected by U.S. travel restrictions. Iran, Ivory Coast and Senegal also face partial or full travel bans.

For Pierrot’s supporters, the World Cup represents more than soccer.

Former Seacoast Soccer Academy coach Corlton Simmond said the tournament still offered an opportunity for connection despite the restrictions.

“We get it, there’s an issue, but the real issue is the game is going to be played, and we’re going to be able to see it and feel the love and be connected with each other,” Simmond said.

Pierrot said the situation gives him and his teammates an even stronger reason to leave it all on the pitch.

“It’s an extra motivation, because we know that there’s a lot of people that would love to travel to see all the games,” Pierrot said. “We know that they would love to be there, and love to be here with us, but we’re going to be with them through their hearts.”

The only exemption to the travel ban applied to the teams participating in the tournament.

Pierrot also announced the launch of a foundation aimed at helping children in Haiti pursue their dreams without having to immigrate to the United States as his parents did.

The June 13 World Cup match in Boston was expected to serve as a homecoming of sorts for the Haitian soccer star.

More on the World Cup

news_communities.title: Former Melrose High School soccer player set to play in the World Cup, for Haiti
news_communities.author: Oscar Margain
news_communities.summary:
A Massachusetts soccer player is getting ready to play for Haiti in the World Cup after helping the team qualify for the tournament for the first time in 52 years.

Frantzdy Pierrot, who played at Melrose High School, moved from Haiti to Massachusetts when he was 11.

“Me playing my first game in Boston means a lot to me,” Pierrot told NBC10 Boston. “It’s like a second World Cup.”

Growing up in Haiti, Pierrot said life often felt uncertain. He said the love from his parents and his passion for soccer gave him stability while he spent his childhood playing soccer with few resources.

“You just play on the streets, and our toes would be bleeding because we’d be kicking rocks,” Pierrot said. “So when I finally got here, I was about to wear shoes, or like, seeing the soccer ball, I was like ‘wow.'”

Pierrot joined youth sports in Medford before playing soccer at Melrose High School, where he met coach Dean Serino.

“He’s the first one that was like, ‘You got something special,'” Pierrot said. “Just him saying that to me made me believe that I can actually do anything.”

Serino said Pierrot’s determination separated him from other players.

“It was his determination that you knew he was going to be great. I knew he was. He was bound for great things, I just didn’t know this great,” Serino said.

Pierrot went on to play at Northeastern University before turning professional. At 31, he now plays soccer in Europe and led Haiti to the World Cup after scoring a qualifying goal against Costa Rica last year.

“I know that I’m giving a lot of people in my country hope,” Pierrot said.

He added that he wanted the people who helped him throughout his journey to feel proud as they watched him compete at the highest level.

“They’re all going to be witnessing me playing on one of the biggest stages, and they can say, ‘This kid, I helped him,'” Pierrot said.

Haiti will face Scotland on June 13 at Boston Stadium. Pierrot will be recognized by Gov. Maura Healey in a proclamation on Tuesday.

news_communities.title: Chelsea plans '39-day party' for World Cup, but still awaits FIFA license
news_communities.author: Oscar Margain
news_communities.summary:
UPDATE (Tuesday, May 26, 2026): The city tells NBC10 Boston that its FIFA license has been approved.

With 20 days remaining before the start of the 2026 FIFA World Cup, the city of Chelsea, Massachusetts, moved ahead with plans for a 39-day celebration despite still waiting for approval to show tournament matches publicly.

City officials launched promotional campaigns for watch parties, soccer events and family activities planned throughout the tournament. Leaders said they expected the World Cup festivities to bring tourism dollars to local businesses while encouraging residents to gather in public again.

Chelsea City Manager Fidel Maltez noted 70% of the city is comprised of a Latino population, and 53% are foreign-born. He said immigration enforcement activity over the last year had hurt businesses and kept many residents from spending time downtown.

“We walked our streets in fear. We felt like we couldn’t come outside. Our business owners complained about how our downtown was decimated,” he said.

Maltez said the city hoped large public gatherings and increased local police presence would help residents feel safe during the tournament.

“Anytime that there was a large gathering, [ICE] shied away from it,” he said.

The city has planned several English and Spanish watch parties in Chelsea Square and other activities tied to the World Cup.

Beginning June 11, Chelsea Square will be transformed into an outdoor gathering space for the tournament. Visitors can expect an outdoor large-screen viewing experience, local food vendors, music and community programming showcasing the cultures and traditions of the city’s diverse community.

Chelsea Sports Tourism Director Charlie Giuffrida said officials wanted to create opportunities for local families and youth soccer players during the tournament.

“We want to make sure our youth soccer ecosystem and all of our community has some amazing things to do to capitalize on what’s going to be, probably, one of the best summers North America’s seen in a long time,” said Giuffrida.

Maltez said the city allocated nearly $500,000 in combined state and local funding for the celebrations.

However, organizers said they still had not received FIFA licensing approval to broadcast matches during public watch parties.

“It’s definitely scary that we’re weeks away and we don’t have a license yet,” said the city’s civic designer, Omar Miranda, who applied for a license several months ago.

Several other cities and towns hosting World Cup watch parties were also still waiting for FIFA approval, including Worcester, Brockton, Everett and Lexington.

Even if the license does not arrive in time, organizers said the city still planned to move forward with festivities.

“They’re going to watch it in our businesses if they have to. We’re just going to have a space for them to have fun and enjoy the celebrations,” said Miranda. “Licenses or not, we’re going to have a 38-, 39-day party here at Chelsea Square.”

The City of Chelsea posted a watch party calendar on its website and is kicking off “Fiesta Fútbol” on Saturday with other public activities to follow:

  • Adult Soccer Tournament: 10:30 a.m. Saturday, May 23, at Highland Park, 45 Willow Street, Chelsea
    The City is accepting 16 teams for an 8v8 double-elimination tournament. Teams are encouraged to play as their home or favorite nations — including Honduras, El Salvador, the United States, Mexico, and others — and wear culturally-themed gear. Pre-registration is required at recreation.chelseama.gov.
  • Soccer Field Day — Youth Soccer Community: 10:30 a.m. – 2:30 p.m. Saturday, June 6 at Highland Park, 45 Willow Street, Chelsea
    Open to the entire Chelsea Youth Soccer Community, Soccer Field Day will feature mini soccer matches, a skills competition, inflatables, local vendors, and more. Families can learn about local soccer programs and register on-site. No pre-registration required.
  • Alumni Soccer Game: 10 a.m. Saturday, June 27 at Veterans Stadium, 299 Everett Ave, Chelsea
    Chelsea soccer alumni ages 18 and older are invited back to the field for a friendly competition and a celebration of community pride. All are welcome, including those who attended private, vocational, or other schools. Players register by graduating year or decade and are grouped accordingly. Games are continuous 7-v-7 format beginning at 10 a.m.

news_communities.title: Annabelle is moving to Salem
news_communities.author: Mary Markos
news_communities.summary:
One of the most infamous dolls in the world is officially coming to Salem, Massachusetts.

The allegedly haunted Annabelle doll, made famous by paranormal investigators Ed and Lorraine Warren, will be part of a new exhibit opening on Essex Street in Salem later this summer.

The announcement came during a livestream walk through downtown Salem by YouTuber Elton Castee and comedian Matt Rife, who now oversee the Warrens’ collection.

Where will the Annabelle exhibit be in Salem?

Castee gave NBC10 Boston an exclusive in-person look at the exterior of the location.

“The part of the building we have is all the way to that corner there … that building … This! Right here!” he said, pointing out the Essex Street space.

The exhibit will take over roughly 4,000 square feet, with plans for around 1,000 to 1,500 artifacts tied to the paranormal and occult history.

The temporary museum is expected to open Aug. 26 and could run through the fall, possibly staying until just before Christmas.

What is the Annabelle doll?

Annabelle is widely known from paranormal investigations and horror films, but its origins trace back decades.

“It is a demonic doll that posed to be this Annabelle Higgins’ spirit,” Castee explained.

According to the Warrens, the doll was believed to be possessed and was ultimately locked away for safety. It has since become one of the most recognizable items in their collection.

Warren Museum collection expanding to Salem

Castee and Rife took over stewardship of the Warren Occult Museum collection in Connecticut and say bringing artifacts to Salem has been months in the making.

“It’s been a process — we’ve been working on this for eight, nine months in Salem alone,” Castee said.

The exhibit will go beyond just Annabelle, featuring a wide range of items connected to:

  • Paranormal investigations
  • Occult history
  • Famous historical cases
  • Cultural beliefs about the supernatural

“We’re not just bringing a few haunted items here,” Castee said. “We’re curating something really expansive.”

Their goal, he says, is to educate visitors — something inspired by the Warrens themselves.

“I want people to leave this experience and be like, ‘We saw Annabelle, but also, I want to look up more about this tribe, or this belief, or these people, or what is this?’ and just leave people with more curiosity than they came in with,” Castee said.

Salem residents react to Annabelle’s arrival

In a city known for its connection to the 1692 Salem Witch Trials and thriving paranormal tourism scene, reaction to Annabelle’s arrival has been mixed — but largely enthusiastic.

“We live in Salem — we’re downtown — this is why we’re famous,” one resident said.

Others say the exhibit fits perfectly with the city’s identity.

“I’m scared — but I feel like it’s also kind of intriguing,” a visitor said.

But there are residents who have raised concerns about late-night crowds.

Museum hours and community impact

City leaders have approved temporary operating hours, allowing the museum to stay open:

  • Until 11 p.m. Sunday through Thursday
  • Until midnight on Fridays and Saturdays

Those hours could be revisited later this year.

Castee says he and his team plan to live in Salem and want to integrate into the community.

“We want to be a part of this community,” he said. “If there’s anything that we can figure out to make things better, please let us know.”

When can you see Annabelle in Salem?

If all goes as planned, visitors will be able to see the Annabelle doll and the expanded exhibit starting late August of this year.

news_communities.title: Boston aims to curb violence in summer months
news_communities.author: Malcolm Johnson
news_communities.summary:
This month alone, Boston has seen a Mother’s Day double shooting in Dorchester, stabbings in Roxbury and East Boston and a fatal shooting in Mattapan.

Now, community leaders and city officials are calling for action ahead of the summer months.

Rev. Pamela Hailey Gilliard of Morningstar Baptist Church said youth programs will be critical in helping prevent violence.

“Now is the time to take action. Our youth are dying right in front of us, and what are we doing to stop it?” Gilliard asked. “What do we have in place? What alternative do we have? Now is the time.”

“We know, overall, the numbers continued to follow a downward trend, and that the statistics this year, to date, actually are a decrease compared to last year,” Mayor Michelle Wu of Boston’s violent crime statistics.

Still, the mayor said that there is work left to be done, any that act of violence is unacceptable.

“We also know that it is not acceptable until there are no incidents of violence whatsoever, and that everyone truly feels safe in every part of our city,” Wu said.

Boston Human Services Chief Jose Masso said families will have access to free and low-cost youth programs and activities throughout the summer.

news_communities.title: Teen charged in Danvers murder appears virtually in court
news_communities.author: Staff Reports
news_communities.summary:
A teenager charged with the murder of a woman in her home in Danvers, Massachusetts, appeared virtually in court Monday.

Anthony DeMayo, 18, of Lynn, is accused of breaking into the home of 68-year-old Janet Swallow on March 12 and stabbing her to death.

DeMayo pleaded not guilty in March to charges of murder and armed home invasion in Salem District Court. He was ordered to undergo mental evaluation at Bridgewater State Hospital.

He remained at the hospital Monday when he was arraigned at Essex Superior Court. While he acknowledged being able to hear the court proceedings, his attorney asked for a plea of not guilty to be entered on his behalf due to questions of competency.

The investigation that led to DeMayo’s arrest began after a 911 caller told police that a man was acting erratically, walking around on Standish Road in Lynn with what looked like a blood-stained knife. Officers responded and found DeMayo, taking him to Salem Hospital.

DeMayo reportedly told Lynn police that he had killed a woman the night before in Danvers.

A search of the Bishop Fenwick High School student’s Lynn home revealed blood-stained clothing, and along with data from his cell phone, investigators contacted the Danvers Police Department, who conducted a well-being check and found Swallow dead inside her home on Amherst Street.

Prosecutors said Swallow was found dead, alone, “suffering wounds consistent with a homicide.” They said DeMayo apparently entered the home by climbing through a window and stabbed Swallow while she was in bed. They said no items were found to be missing from the home and they believe the attack was random.

The longtime Danvers resident was an ICU nurse at Lahey Hospital in Burlington and leaves behind two sons.

Scott James, Lahey’s chief nursing officer, called Swallow “a beloved and valued member of the Lahey Hospital & Medical Center community” who will be “greatly missed.”

“We share our deepest condolences with her family and loved ones and her colleagues in the Nursing Department and across our hospital. Our thoughts are with them during this very difficult time,” he added.

Officials said there is no known connection between DeMayo and Swallow.

DeMayo remained held without bail after Monday’s superior court hearing.

A status hearing is scheduled for May 29.

news_communities.title: Fallen Trooper Kevin Trainor's mother: ‘I just remember screaming for my son'
news_communities.author: Robert Goulston
news_communities.summary:
The grieving mother of fallen Massachusetts State Police Trooper Kevin Trainor visited the barracks where he worked to spend time inside his cruiser on Monday, days before his funeral.

Barbara Trainor said the cruiser covered in flowers was her son’s police cruiser for the first few years on the job. She wanted to spend time in it before it was moved.

“It just made us feel a connection to him,” said Trainor. “Try to see what he was going through every day. One of the troopers explaining everything to us, which was really nice.

Trooper Trainor was killed early Wednesday morning while responding to a report of a wrong-way driver on route one in Lynnfield. The Essex County District Attorney’s office says the wrong-way driver hit Trainor’s current cruiser head-on. Both Trainor and the other driver were killed.

“It just keeps replaying in my mind over and over and over. How he was almost there and he would be here today,” Trainor said in an interview.

Trainor’s mother said she got the phone call minutes after it happened: “I got a call in the middle of the night and I answered the phone. I just literally blacked out. I just remember screaming for my son.”

Trainor says she will never forget that knock on the door, “and saw them on my porch. I knew what that meant and I just collapsed. Literally collapsed.”

More on the death of Trooper Kevin Trainor

Massachusetts May 11

Funeral details for Mass. State Police trooper killed in wrong-way crash

Massachusetts State Police May 8

‘Heartbreaking': Trooper and other driver mourned after wrong-way crash

Massachusetts State Police May 7

‘The best of the best': Trooper Trainor hailed as hero after death in line of duty

Trooper Trainor’s funeral Mass will be at the same church in Salem where he just gave the eulogy for his father back in March.

“We remember now in a moment like this to honor those who bravely put themselves in danger for our safety,” said Father Bob Murray, pastor at Mary, Queen of the Apostles Parish in Salem.

Murray will also preside at Trooper Trainor’s funeral Mass on Wednesday.

“His sense of what it meant to serve and to be a man at the beginning of his career, to serve with the Middlesex sheriff and then to move on to the state troopers,” said Murray.

Trainor’s mother said she knows her son and his father are together and knows her son’s spirit will live on.

“Nothing I can even say about that. Constant sadness. I miss my son,” Trainor said.

Trooper Trainor’s original cruiser has been moved from outside the barracks to be prepared for the funeral arrangements later this week. The wake is scheduled for Tuesday and funeral on Wednesday.

news_communities.title: State trooper dies after wrong-way crash that closed Route 1 north in Lynnfield
news_communities.author: Kaitlin McKinley Becker
news_communities.summary:
A Massachusetts State Police trooper died from his injuries after his cruiser was struck by a wrong-way driver, who also died, on Route 1 in Lynnfield early Wednesday, officials said.

State police confirmed the trooper’s death, saying they did so was with deep sadness and condolences to the trooper’s family, classmates and loved ones.

Gov. Maura Healey said she was heartbroken by the trooper’s tragic death, that her thoughts and prayers were with his family, loved ones and fellow state police members during this incredibly difficult time.

“The Trooper was responding to reports of a wrong-way driver and put himself in harm’s way to protect others,” Healey wrote in a statement. “This act of service and courage is what our State Police are called to do every day. They show up ready to face danger so the rest of us can be safe. Today, Massachusetts is mourning one of those heroes.”

State police identified the trooper at a press conference outside the Danvers barracks as 30-year-old Kevin Trainor, a trooper of three years who represented “the best of the best.” A procession was held for him in the South End, as fellow first responders saluted as his body was taken by.

Click here for more comments about Trainor from the press conference

State police said earlier Wednesday that the trooper had been rushed to a Boston hospital with life-threatening injuries after his cruiser was struck around 2 a.m. by a driver who was traveling south in the northbound lanes on Route 1 northbound.

Trainor’s shift was scheduled to end at 2 a.m., according to state police. The 911 call reporting a wrong-way driver came in at 2:01 a.m., and Trainor’s cruiser was struck head-on at 2:04 a.m.

The wrong-way driver, identified by Essex County prosecutors as Hernan Marrero, a 50-year-old Roslindale resident, died at the scene, while Trainor succumbed to his injuries at Massachusetts General Hospital.

“While nothing will ever repair the pain we feel today, we make a solemn promise to never forget Trooper Trainor and his service to the Commonwealth,” Col. Geoffrey Noble said in a statement. “Make no mistake: Trooper Trainor saved lives this morning. His courage – his commitment to serve the citizens of the Commonwealth and put his life on the line – prevented an even greater tragedy. He will be remembered as a hero.”

“We will remember and honor this extraordinary man, son, fiancée, and friend who gave his life for others,” Noble continued.

The Massachusetts Department of Transportation announced Route 1 northbound was closed at Walnut Street in Saugus due to the crash, and traffic was being detoured.

Drivers were told to seek alternate routes, and anyone traveling through the impacted area was warned to expect delays, MassDOT said. Closures lasted several hours.

news_communities.title: Child dies after tree falls on playground outside Melrose school
news_communities.author: Thea DiGiammerino and Malcolm Johnson
news_communities.summary:
A child has died after a tree fell on a playground outside an elementary school in Melrose, Massachusetts, on Monday, injuring three people.

Fire officials said two children and an adult were hurt in the incident at Winthrop Elementary School. Melrose Public Schools confirmed in a letter to families that a student injured in the incident died.

“This is a very sad day at Winthrop School and for our entire Melrose Community.  Our deepest condolences go out to the family, and we ask that you please respect their privacy at this time,” a letter signed by Superintendent Cari Berman and Mayor Jennifer Grigoraitis reads.

The person who died hasn’t been publicly identified, but families in the district said they remembered the child fondly. A memorial for the student was growing in town.

Aerial footage from the scene on Monday showed a downed tree at a playground. First responders could be seen in the parking lot, which was blocked by police tape.

The superintendent and mayor’s letter added that the other two people have been released from the hospital. Tuesday’s Melrose School Committee meeting was canceled.

Counselors will be available at the Winthrop School to support students and staff.

The Melrose Educators Union released a statement saying, “Today, our entire school community is grieving the tragic loss of a young student. We are heartbroken for the family and for all who knew and loved them.”

news_communities.title: New details revealed on Saugus home invasion where gunshots were fired
news_communities.author: Robert Goulston, Asher Klein and Mary Markos
news_communities.summary:
Prosecutors revealed new information on Sunday’s armed home invasion in Saugus, Massachusetts, as the suspects appeared in court Monday.

Derek Matarazzo and Timothy Gregory are charged with multiple charges including home invasion. Matarazzo is also charged with assault with a dangerous weapon and discharging a firearm.

On Tuesday in Lynn District Court, both suspects were ordered held without bail pending a dangerousness hearing.

Police say the two men who were dressed all in black armed with gun, and then when they arrived at the home, the two men were found in the yard. Gunshots were fired, but no one was hurt.

Prosecutors say both men were out of breath and Matarazzo was asking if the other suspect was okay.

“Making statements about potentially going to jail for 30 years and asking officers if his friends was dead or shot,” said Essex Assistant District Attorney Kaitlyn Bastarache said.

The police reports were impounded until after a grand jury hears the case, but the prosecutor indicated in court the men were after safes in the house and that the attempted robbery was caught on video.

“There is mention of surveillance video in the home that was broken into, in the bedroom in which items were taken, has video from inside the home as well,” Bastarache said.

Several people were in court on behalf of two suspects. One woman would only say she was a friend of Matarazzo’s family.

“He made a few mistakes. His family is behind him. They are working really hard to get him back,” said the woman. “He is going into really work through this and his friends and family are all behind him.”

We did talk to one person who was in the home at the time of the incident. He said they were all shaken up and there was a young person in the home at the time. But made it clear he did not want to be identified or do an interview at this time.

news_communities.title: 2 men arrested after armed home invasion with shots fired in Saugus, police say
news_communities.author: Asher Klein and Mary Markos
news_communities.summary:
Gunshots were fired in a daytime armed home invasion in Saugus, Massachusetts, on Sunday, police say, and the two suspects are in custody.

No one was hurt in the shooting on Oakwood Avenue about noon, Saugus police said. Two Boston men, Derek Matarazzo and Timothy Gregory, are facing felony charges including home invasion after their arrest shortly after the 911 calls came in.

The calls reported two men in masks, dressed in black, armed with guns, breaking into a house, police said. They didn’t share what led to the gunfire or how the men were tracked down, saying only that the department wasn’t speculating on their motivation.

Matarazzo and Gregory are believed to be the only people directly involved in the home invasion, police said, and it’s believed to be an isolated incident, so there’s no danger to the public.

Neighbors who spoke with NBC10 Boston say they are shaken up by what occurred, describing a shootout right outside their homes in the middle of the day.

Ring camera video from a nearby home shows the aftermath, as neighbors say you can see the homeowner running into the middle of the street with a phone pressed to his ear, desperately flagging down police — after the chaos.

A neighbor tells us his family first heard what sounded like a pop — something they thought could’ve been a lawn mower backfiring, until they realized it was gunfire. That neighbor says one of his daughters then saw a man carrying a safe — dropping it in their front yard — while shots were being fired.

“I saw somebody come out of the house shooting and then we all hit the deck, because you didn’t want a stray bullet to ricochet off something and come through the window or anything like that,” George Benn said.

“I saw the shots. I saw a man go down. I thought he was going to be dead but apparently he just flipped on that hill,” Tom Bushee said.

The investigation is ongoing.

news_communities.title: Woman injured after jumping from 2nd story of burning building in Everett
news_communities.author: Marc Fortier and Amelia Fabiano
news_communities.summary:
A woman was injured after jumping from the second story of a burning building in Everett, Massachusetts, on Friday, fire officials said.

The fire was reported around 11 a.m. on Franklin Street. When firefighters arrived, they found fire in the rear hallway of the building going up to the second and third floor.

Fire officials said they received a report that someone was still trapped inside on the second floor, but she jumped out a window before firefighters were able to extend a ladder to her. She suffered a head injury and was taken to an area hospital. No update on her condition was immediately available.

Neighbor Billy Hurley Jr. said he saw the woman leaning out of the window, with thick black smoke billowing around her. After hesitating, she made the jump.

“Either you’re gonna live or you’re gonna die, so she jumped out the window and she missed a fence,” Hurley said, adding he “could hear the thud of it.”

Everyone else made it out of the building safely, officials said. Fifteen residents were displaced in all.

Two firefighters suffered non-life-threatening injuries while battling the fire.

The fire took about an hour to extinguish, and the cause remains under investigation. State Fire Marshal’s Office investigators also responded to the scene.

“I’m a lifelong neighbor on the street, all my family’s been on the street over 107 years, so it was sad to see the whole house that’s been around … catch on fire and there’s so many people displaced,” Hurley said.

news_communities.title: State Ethics Commission alleges misconduct by former Everett Mayor Carlo DeMaria
news_communities.author: Glenn Jones and Mike Pescaro
news_communities.summary:
The former mayor of Everett is facing renewed scrutiny from the Massachusetts State Ethics Commission.

Last February, Massachusetts Inspector General Jeffrey Shapiro found that then-Mayor Carlo DeMaria had erroneously received an extra $180,000 in longevity payments.

DeMaria, who served 18 years as mayor, lost his reelection bid to new Mayor Robert Van Campen in November in the wake of the scandal.

The State Ethics Commission opened an investigation earlier this month and said Wednesday that it had found DeMaria violated the state’s conflict of interest law.

“DeMaria violated § 23(b)(2)(i) by using his official position as Mayor of Everett to conceal his receipt of unauthorized and inflated longevity payments, which was an unwarranted privilege of substantial value not properly available to similarly situated individuals, and he did so repeatedly and continuously while mayor,” the commission wrote.

Shapiro’s office said that before the ordinance granting longevity payments was approved in 2016, a subtle tweak was made to its language: Instead of saying the mayor would receive $10,000 “after each completed full term as mayor,” as it previously did, it was amended to read “for each completed full term as mayor.”

Ultimately, DeMaria was overpaid by $180,000, Shapiro said.

An independent audit later found DeMaria had received an additional $30,000 in improper funds.

The former mayor has not been penalized for the payments.

NBC10 Boston reached out to DeMaria for comment. He has previously denied any wrongdoing.

The State Ethics Commission is authorized to impose a civil penalty of up to $10,000 for each violation and may also order restitution payments. It will schedule a hearing within 90 days.

news_communities.title: 2 arrested in Everett bank robbery as Boston police investigate 2 more
news_communities.author: Matt Fortin, Asher Klein and Mike Pescaro
news_communities.summary:
A bank was robbed in Everett, Massachusetts, hours after two other bank robberies in Boston Tuesday, and two people have been arrested, police said.

Everett police initially said the suspects taken into custody after the robbery of Eastern Bank at the intersection of Broadway and Ferry Street, shortly after 4:30 p.m., matched the description of the two being sought by Boston police. They later said the people who were arrested were not the same ones wanted in the Boston crimes.

A Boston police representative previously said the department was aware of the arrests in Everett, but didn’t know of any confirmed connection.

Police have not identified the two people who were arrested.

On Tuesday morning, police in Boston said two separate robberies occurred in Roxbury and Roslindale, and that the same people were believed to be responsible for both crimes.

“Both of today’s robberies are very active investigations. Preliminary information gathered by detectives indicates both robberies involve the same suspects,” Boston Police Sgt. Detective John Boyle said in an email after the second robbery.

No injuries were reported in either case.

Everett police later responded to the robbery at Eastern Bank. They said a female robbed the bank before meeting up with a male and walking up Broadway and onto Gledhill Avenue, where police stopped them.

A gun and a machete were recovered, police said.

Boston police had released surveillance images from the two robberies in that city as they searched for the robbers.

The first robbery occurred at a Santander Bank branch on Washington Street in the Nubian Square area, and photos showed someone brandishing a gun at a teller about 10:11 a.m.

The two male robbers inside the bank wore blue face masks, blue gloves, hoodies and black pants. Police said they ran off along Harrison Avenue toward Orchard Park, and might have been in a blue Toyota RAV4 with Massachusetts license plate 4MXN78.

The second robbery was reported around 11:55 a.m. at a TD Bank branch on American Legion Highway. Two people robbed the bank at gunpoint, stealing an undisclosed amount of money, then ran out to a waiting car being driven by a third person. They drove off toward Cummins Highway, police said.

The first suspect is described as a male wearing a black hoodie, black pants, a black face mask, white and black sneakers, and blue gloves. The second suspect is described as a male wearing a black Champion hoodie with white lettering, black pants, and a black face mask.

As with the first incident, the suspects fled the scene in a blue Toyota RAV4 with Massachusetts license plate 4MXN78.

news_communities.title: Beloved North Shore ice cream spot closes after 74 years in business
news_communities.author: Boston Restaurant Talk
news_communities.summary:
[This story first appeared on Boston Restaurant Talk.]

A North Shore ice cream spot that has been around for a long time has shut down.

According to multiple sources, Dairy Witch in Salem will not be reopening for the season, with a Facebook post from the Boston Street spot saying the following:

After 74 years, Dairy Witch is closing. That is not an easy sentence to type. There is a weight to it. Bea and Pete Polemenako opened the window at 117 Boston Street in 1952, the same year their daughter Marietta was born. Marietta has stood by that window her entire life, but will be retiring this year. We will not be opening this spring. There will not be another season….We’ll be sharing more in the coming days. Old photos, stories, and a few things we’ve been working on, so a piece of Dairy Witch can stick around even after the window stays closed. For now we just wanted Salem to hear it from us before anyone else. Thank you.

Dairy Witch has been known over the years for its variety of ice cream flavors along with its sundaes, banana splits, frappes, freezes, malts, and more.

(Follow Marc on Bluesky at @marchurboston.bsky.social)

news_communities.title: Road rage fight caught on camera in Lynn
news_communities.author: Oscar Margain and Matt Fortin
news_communities.summary:
A man was hospitalized after a road rage incident Friday morning in Lowell, Massachusetts.

The incident escalated into an assault at a car wash on Route 1A. It’s being investigated by Massachusetts State Police, who responded shortly before 9:30 a.m..

Video from the scene showed traffic at a standstill as several people got out of their cars and started fighting in the roadway.

Two men were seen throwing punches while others tried to break it up.

A third man can be seen knocking someone to the ground.

“People have an ego,” said Jay Lopez, a customer at the car wash. “When somebody doesn’t back down, they have like, ‘Oh, I’m not going back down either.’ And then they just have to go back and forth until somebody goes to sleep.”

He feared it could have gone worse.

“It could have been a gun,” he said. “All you’ve got to do is mind your business. Drink water, mind your business.”

State police said the man who was knocked down suffered a laceration. He was taken to an area hospital.

No arrests have been made.

The case remains under investigation.

news_communities.title: 3 charged with human trafficking at Salem spas
news_communities.summary:
Police in Salem, Massachusetts, said three people are facing charges after a monthslong human trafficking investigation into several businesses.

Authorities said they investigated suspected trafficking and prostitution-related activities at businesses advertising “bodywork,” “reflexology” and “spa” services over the last three months.

Detectives executed search warrants Friday at businesses including New Custom Body Work and Red Rose Reflexology, both on Boston Street, police said Wednesday. They did not say whether more businesses were searched.

Salem police said 57-year-old Peter Leavitt of Peabody, 52-year-old Fang Liu of Peabody and 64-year-old Barry St. Onge of Shrewsbury were taken into custody and will be charged with trafficking of a person for sexual servitude, deriving support from prostitution and keeping a house of prostitution.

The Salem Police Department noted that human trafficking “involves the recruitment, harboring, transportation, provision, or obtaining of individuals for the purposes of forced labor or commercial exploitation.” It said in a press release that it “emphasizes that individuals involved in these situations are regarded as victims and are treated accordingly throughout the investigative process.”

Authorities did not say when the suspects would be arraigned or if they had attorneys.

“We see this as much a rescue operation as it is a criminal investigation,” Salem Police Chief Lucas Miller said in a statement. “The exploitation of victims of human trafficking will not be tolerated in the city of Salem.”

news_communities.title: East Boston getting popular gourmet doughnut/sandwich shop
news_communities.author: Boston Restaurant Talk
news_communities.summary:
[This story first appeared on Boston Restaurant Talk.]

The team behind a handful of gourmet doughnut shops and sandwich shops in the Boston area will be bringing the two concepts to a waterfront location.

According to a Facebook reel from Gallows Group founder Rebecca Roth Gullo, Blackbird Doughnuts and Sally’s Sandwiches will be opening in East Boston, moving into a space at Clippership Wharf. In the reel, Rebecca mentions that there will also be a “little sprinkling of The Gallows,” which was a gastropub that resided in Boston’s South End until closing in 2021, and that the business plans to open sometime in the late spring or early summer.

Locations of Blackbird Doughnuts can be found in Boston’s South End, Beacon Hill, Fenway, Brighton, Cambridge’s Harvard Square, Newton Centre, and Watertown, while Sally’s Sandwiches can be found in the South End, Harvard Square, and Brighton. The website for all locations can be found at blackbirddoughnuts.com.

news_communities.title: Chelsea High School teacher on leave after allegations of inappropriate behavior
news_communities.author: Griffin Gonzalez
news_communities.summary:
A high school teacher in Chelsea, Massachusetts, has been placed on leave after allegedly having inappropriate conversations with students.

Parents and students say the inappropriate behavior went on for years. The district took action after students staged a walkout at Chelsea High School this week.

“For the past three years, I’ve been enduring a teacher who has been inappropriate towards myself and other students,” 17-year-old Jelannie Hunter told the Chelsea School Committee this week.

“When you’re working with children, there are boundaries,” her mother told NBC10 Boston in an interview Thursday.

She said she went to the school for the past three years complaining of the teacher’s conversations with her daughter.

Students at Chelsea High School walked out of class Wednesday to protest the lack of action taken by the district despite complaints from students and parents.

“As of today, the employee involved has been placed on leave,” Chelsea Public Schools said in a letter to the Chelsea High School community Wednesday. “The school district has also initiated an independent investigation of the concerns raised regarding this matter.”

The district also said in the letter that while it “encourages and supports students’ constitutional rights to freedom of speech and expression,” it planned to discipline some students who stayed in the halls longer and “and disrupted the instructional day for themselves and others who did not participate.”

NBC10 Boston is not naming the teacher, as no charges have been filed against her.

We reached out to the district to see whether or not she would be paid throughout her leave and why action was taken now after years of complaint, but have not received a response.

news_communities.title: As Artemis II crew splashes down, Navy sailor from Mass. was set to greet them
news_communities.author: Thea DiGiammerino
news_communities.summary:
A U.S. Navy sailor from Massachusetts was expected to be among the first people to greet the Artemis II astronauts when they returned to Earth Friday evening.

Chief Hospital Corpsman Vlad Link is a member of the dive medical team that was set to open the Orion capsule, the U.S. Navy said in a media release. Link, who is from Chelsea, was expected to help get the astronauts out of the capsule safely after splashdown and make an initial medical assessment of their condition.

Link has 18 years of experience in dive medicine.

“I have been exposed to the Navy since I was a young teenager, and I’m proud to represent both my family and hometown,” Link said in the media release. “Contributing our efforts to NASA and the Artemis II mission is something we take great pride in as part of that legacy.”

NASA astronauts Reid Wiseman, Christina Koch and Victor Glover and Canadian astronaut Jeremy Hansen returned to Earth on Friday evening after 10 days in space. This involved a fiery plunge through the atmosphere, ending in a splashdown in the Pacific Ocean off the coast of San Diego.
